system_design.label: System Design - Large-Scale Services
system_design.description: Large-scale system design case studies, capacity estimation, technical depth in service design.

q1.label: Capacity Estimation
q1.description: How do you estimate capacity requirements for a service?

metadata.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["System Design Basics"]
    B["Scalability"]
    C["Availability"]
    D["Consistency"]
    E["Trade-offs"]
    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    B --> E
    C --> E
    D --> E
    style A fill:#ffebee
    style B fill:#ffcdd2
    style C fill:#ef9a9a
    style D fill:#e57373
    style E fill:#ef5350
metadata.answer:
## Capacity Estimation Framework

Capacity estimation calculates required resources (servers, storage, bandwidth) based on projected user growth and usage patterns.

### Key Steps

1. **Estimate Daily Active Users (DAU)**
   - Research market penetration: 100M registered → 10% DAU = 10M DAU

2. **Estimate QPS (Queries Per Second)**
   - Formula: (DAU × requests/user per day) / 86400
   - Example: 10M users × 100 requests = 1.15M QPS avg, 3-4× peak

3. **Calculate Storage**
   - Per user storage × users × retention years
   - Example: 10M users × 1 MB = 10 TB + 3× replication = 30 TB

4. **Estimate Bandwidth**
   - Peak QPS × avg bytes/request with 3-5× peak multiplier
   - Example: 3M QPS × 1 KB = 3 GB/s ≈ 24 Gbps

5. **Add Redundancy & Growth Buffer**
   - 2-3× replication, 2× growth buffer for 6-12 months

### Trade-offs
- **Overestimate**: Waste money, unused capacity
- **Underestimate**: Degradation, cascading failures
- **Solution**: Quarterly incremental planning

q2.label: Service Design - TinyURL
q2.description: Design a URL shortening service. Handle scale, caching, database choice.

metadata.answer:
## TinyURL System Design

### Requirements
- Shorten long URLs to ~7 character codes
- Redirect from short to original with 301 Moved Permanently
- Optional custom codes and expiration

### Database
```
URLs: id, long_url, short_url (unique), user_id, created_at, expires_at, click_count
Analytics: id, url_id, user_agent, ip, timestamp
```

### Base62 Encoding
- 62 characters (0-9, a-z, A-Z) = 62^7 = 3.5T unique URLs
- Convert ID to base62 string

### Cache: Redis
- Cache-aside pattern, hot URLs only (Pareto)
- Bidirectional: shortUrl↔longUrl
- TTL: 1 hour

### Scaling
- Shard by user_id or URL hash
- Read replicas for analytics
- CDN for redirects at edge
- Async batch cleanup of expired

### Capacity: 1M URLs/day, 1B redirects/day (12K avg, 50K peak QPS), 100GB/year

metadata.answer:
## Pastebin System Design

### Features
- Post text/code snippets with unique URLs
- Public/private access control
- Optional expiration
- Language syntax highlighting

### Database
```
Pastes: id, user_id, content (compressed), language, expires_at, view_count, is_public
Users: id, username, created_at
```

### Storage Tiers
- Hot (1 month): Database
- Warm (1 year): S3
- Cold (7+ years): Glacier
- Compression: gzip 70-80% reduction

### Caching
- Redis: Top 1% viewed pastes
- TTL: 24 hours

### Scaling
- Shard by paste_id or user_id
- 2-3× replication
- Chunked upload for large pastes
- CDN for syntax JS/CSS

### Capacity: 1M pastes/day, 5KB avg, 5TB/year with compression

metadata.answer:
## Instagram System Design

### Core: Feed generation (millions of photos), photo storage (1-5 MB), real-time notifications

### Database
```
Users: id, username, email, bio, followers_count
Posts: id, user_id, caption, image_url, created_at, like_count
Likes: post_id, user_id (unique constraint)
Follows: follower_id, following_id
```

### Photo Storage
- Cloud: S3/GCS for originals
- CDN: CloudFront for distribution
- Multi-sizes: Original, thumb (200×200), feed (640×640)
- Lazy generation: Generate on-demand, cache in CDN

### Feed Generation
- Hybrid push/pull: Celebs use pull, regulars use push
- Cache in Redis (sorted by timestamp)
- Background pre-compute
- Async fanout to follower feeds

### Real-time Notifications
- Pub/Sub (Kafka/RabbitMQ): like, comment, follow, mention
- Websocket/polling for delivery
- Store in database for history

### Caching
- Redis: Profiles, followers, hot feeds
- Memcached: Post metadata, like counts

### Capacity: 1M posts/day, 500M DAU, 5B likes/day, 2MB/post (3 versions), 1.8PB/year (2-year retention)

behavioral_star_format.label: STAR Format Mastery
behavioral_star_format.description: Master the STAR (Situation, Task, Action, Result) framework - the structured approach to answering behavioral interview questions with compelling stories.

q1.label: Tell me about a conflict you resolved
q1.description: Describe a situation where you had a disagreement with a colleague or manager. How did you handle it?

metadata.answer: Situation: At my previous company, I proposed refactoring our payment system architecture, but the tech lead disagreed - he wanted to patch the existing system. Task: As a senior engineer, I was responsible for system reliability. Action: Instead of overruling him, I scheduled a design review meeting. I presented data showing our current approach would hit limits in 6 months. I asked for his concerns and listened. We found common ground - a phased approach. Result: We implemented the refactor over 3 sprints. It reduced bugs by 40% and onboarding time for new engineers from 2 weeks to 3 days. Learning: Disagreement isn't bad - it forces better decisions when you listen.

q2.label: Describe a time you failed and what you learned
q2.description: Tell me about a project or task that didn't go as planned. What was the failure and how did you recover?

metadata.answer: Situation: I led a migration from monolith to microservices at my startup. Task: I owned the technical strategy and timeline. Action: I underestimated complexity and set aggressive deadlines. We launched with performance issues - p99 latency was 5 seconds instead of 500ms. I took ownership immediately. Instead of blaming the team, I: (1) Rolled back to partial rollout, (2) Instrumented to find bottlenecks, (3) Found N+1 query issues and optimized caching, (4) Relaunched 2 weeks later. Result: Successfully completed migration, learned to add 50% buffer to estimates. Communicated delays transparently with stakeholders. Team trust actually increased because they saw me own the mistake. Learning: Failure is a teacher - what matters is ownership and recovery.

q3.label: Give an example of your leadership
q3.description: Tell me about a time you led without formal authority, or influenced others to follow your vision

metadata.answer: Situation: Our team had no on-call rotation - incidents were handled ad-hoc, people were burned out. Task: As a senior engineer (not a manager), I needed to improve this. Action: I didn't mandate - I proposed. I researched on-call best practices, created a 1-page proposal with: benefits (predictability, fewer burnouts), process (rotating weekly), and support (runbooks, mentoring). I volunteered to own the first rotation. I mentored junior engineers through incidents, didn't just hand off the pages. I gathered feedback weekly and iterated. Result: Within 2 months, 100% participation. Incident MTTR dropped 30%. Team satisfaction improved visibly. 3 engineers thanked me for the structure. Learning: Leadership is about influence through credibility and service, not titles.

q4.label: Tell me about an innovation or improvement you drove
q4.description: Describe a time you proposed or implemented a new idea that had significant impact

metadata.answer: Situation: Our deployment process was manual - engineers ran 15 steps to release, took 45 minutes, high error rate. Task: As a platform engineer, I owned deployment reliability. Action: I built a deployment automation system using GitHub Actions + custom scripts. I: (1) Documented current process (found 3 error-prone steps), (2) Built automation over 2 sprints, (3) Tested thoroughly with dry runs, (4) Trained team on new process, (5) Kept manual fallback for safety. Result: Deployment time dropped from 45 min to 8 min. Error rate dropped to near zero. Freed up engineers for feature work. 50+ deployments with zero failures in first month. Learning: Innovation isn't about greenfield - it's about identifying friction and removing it. Also, don't let perfect be enemy of good - kept manual fallback for psychological safety.

q5.label: Describe a collaboration challenge and how you handled it
q5.description: Tell me about working across teams or with difficult people to achieve a goal

metadata.answer: Situation: Building a real-time notification system required coordination: backend (me), frontend, mobile, and analytics teams. Each had different priorities and tight deadlines. Task: I owned the backend API design and had to satisfy 3 teams' needs. Action: Instead of designing in isolation, I: (1) Set up weekly syncs with each team lead, (2) Created shared technical spec, (3) Built mock API so teams could start in parallel, (4) Provided SDKs for each platform, (5) Held weekly integration reviews. The mobile team had a tight deadline - I prioritized their requests and shipped early. Result: All teams launched within 1 week of each other. Mobile team shipped on time. API became the standard for other services. Teams asked me to lead architecture for next feature. Learning: Collaboration isn't about you being right - it's about shared understanding and removing blockers for others.

q6.label: Tell me when you had to adapt quickly to change
q6.description: Describe a situation where plans changed and you had to pivot. How did you handle it?

metadata.answer: Situation: We had a feature 80% complete, planned for launch in 2 weeks. Customer feedback showed we misunderstood their core need - the feature solved wrong problem. Task: I was the tech lead. We had to either ship wrong thing or pivot. Action: I recommended pivot despite sunk cost. I: (1) Analyzed what we could salvage (30% of code reusable), (2) Redesigned with customer input, (3) Re-estimated: 1 week vs 2 weeks, (4) Brought stakeholders into decision (transparency), (5) Mobilized team for 1-week sprint. Result: Shipped pivoted feature in 1 week. Solved actual customer problem. Feature adoption was 5x higher than originally estimated. Customer expanded engagement with us. Learning: Sunk cost fallacy is real - ego makes you want to ship wrong thing. Better to adapt early than ship and iterate. Also, being transparent about why lets team move fast without resentment.

q7.label: Show me technical depth - a complex technical problem you solved
q7.description: Tell me about the hardest technical challenge you faced. How did you approach it?

metadata.answer: Situation: Our database was hitting scaling limits - queries were slow, replication was falling behind. We had 100K concurrent users and growing. Task: I owned database optimization. We couldn't just add more servers - cost would explode. Action: I: (1) Profiled queries with EXPLAIN ANALYZE - found N+1 queries and missing indexes, (2) Denormalized one large table to reduce joins, (3) Implemented caching layer (Redis) for frequently accessed data, (4) Sharded user data by region, (5) Built monitoring to catch regressions. For the sharding: I wrote a migration strategy that was zero-downtime - dual-write for 2 weeks, then switched traffic. Result: Query latency dropped from 500ms p99 to 50ms. Replication lag went from 5 seconds to <100ms. Supported 10x growth without adding infrastructure cost. System stayed up during peak loads. Learning: Scaling is about identifying bottlenecks, not just throwing hardware. Understanding tradeoffs - denormalization vs joins, caching consistency, sharding hotspots - that's where technical depth matters.

behavioral_competencies.label: Behavioral Competencies - What Interviewers Look For
behavioral_competencies.description: Understand the 7 key competencies that behavioral interviews assess. What does each mean? What are interviewers looking for? What makes a strong answer?

q1.label: What is Conflict Resolution and what do interviewers look for?
q1.description: Explain the conflict resolution competency. What does it measure? What makes a strong answer?

metadata.answer: Conflict Resolution measures: Can you handle disagreement professionally? Do you stay calm under tension? Can you find win-win solutions? Do you listen to other perspectives? Interviewers look for: (1) You taking ownership of finding resolution, not blaming. (2) Listening to the other person's viewpoint, not just defending yours. (3) Problem-solving approach - what specifically did you do? (4) Outcome that strengthened relationship, not just resolved issue. (5) Learning - what would you do differently? Red flags: 'I was right and they came around', 'I escalated to my manager', 'I avoided them'. Green flags: 'I asked why they disagreed', 'We found a third option neither of us thought of', 'We both learned something'.
metadata.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Topic"]
    B["Concept"]
    C["Application"]
    D["Best Practice"]
    E["Expertise"]
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E
    style A fill:#eceff1
    style E fill:#455a64

q2.label: What is Failure & Recovery and what do interviewers look for?
q2.description: Explain the failure and recovery competency. What does it reveal? What makes a strong answer?

metadata.answer: Failure & Recovery measures: Do you learn from mistakes? Can you take responsibility without excuses? Do you recover quickly? Do you prevent recurrence? Interviewers look for: (1) Clear admission of fault - 'I made a mistake because...', not 'The team...' (2) Understanding root cause - why did it happen? (3) Swift action to fix - did you panic or think clearly? (4) Communication - did you hide it or tell stakeholders? (5) Prevention - what changed so it won't happen again? (6) Growth mindset - 'This taught me...'. Red flags: 'I didn't really fail, it wasn't my fault', 'We're still dealing with it', 'I don't remember'. Green flags: 'The failure cost $X and I took responsibility', 'I prevented similar failures', 'It changed how I approach work'.

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E

q3.label: What is Leadership (without authority) and what do interviewers look for?
q3.description: Explain leadership competency. What counts as leadership if you're not a manager? What makes a strong answer?

metadata.answer: Leadership (without authority) measures: Can you influence without power? Do people follow your vision? Can you move teams in a direction? Interviewers look for: (1) Identifying a problem nobody else is solving. (2) Taking initiative - did you wait for permission or just start? (3) Building credibility - why did people listen to you? (4) Getting buy-in - did you convince or force? (5) Enabling others - did you empower them or hoard credit? (6) Results - did your initiative ship, create value? Red flags: 'I proposed something but others didn't agree', 'The team wasn't ready', 'I told them what to do'. Green flags: 'I led 3 engineers through a technical migration', 'People started following the process I created', 'Other teams asked me to lead their initiatives'.

q4.label: What is Innovation and what do interviewers look for?
q4.description: Explain the innovation competency. What counts as innovation? What makes a strong answer?

metadata.answer: Innovation measures: Can you identify problems and create solutions? Do you think beyond constraints? Can you convince others your idea is worth it? Interviewers look for: (1) Problem identification - what friction did you see? (2) Original thinking - why hadn't others solved it? (3) Feasibility - was your idea pie-in-the-sky or practical? (4) Execution - did you build it or just propose it? (5) Impact - did it actually matter? Metrics? (6) Adoption - did others use it? Red flags: 'I had a great idea but nobody believed in me', 'I built it but people didn't want it', 'It was too complex'. Green flags: 'I shipped a feature saving engineers 2 hours/week', 'Other teams copied my approach', 'Usage grew 10x month-over-month'.

q5.label: What is Collaboration and what do interviewers look for?
q5.description: Explain the collaboration competency. What does it really measure? What makes a strong answer?

metadata.answer: Collaboration measures: Can you work effectively with others? Do you communicate well? Can you handle diverse perspectives? Do you unblock people? Interviewers look for: (1) Initiative to communicate - did you proactively coordinate or wait for sync? (2) Listening - did you understand others' constraints? (3) Flexibility - were you rigid or adaptable? (4) Removing blockers - did you help others succeed? (5) Humility - did you admit when you were wrong? (6) Outcome focus - did the team ship better because you collaborated? Red flags: 'I did my part, others did theirs', 'They didn't understand my technical approach', 'It was hard to work with X'. Green flags: 'I spent time understanding their constraints', 'We shipped faster because we aligned early', 'They asked me to collaborate on next project'.

q6.label: What is Adaptability and what do interviewers look for?
q6.description: Explain the adaptability competency. What makes someone adaptable? What makes a strong answer?

metadata.answer: Adaptability measures: Can you handle change? Do you stay productive when plans shift? Can you learn quickly? Do you stay composed? Interviewers look for: (1) Acceptance - did you fight change or embrace it? (2) Problem-solving - how did you figure out new situation? (3) Speed - how fast did you adjust? (4) Communication - did you ask questions or assume? (5) Outcome - did you still deliver? (6) Attitude - did you learn or just survive? Red flags: 'The requirements changed and we missed deadline', 'I didn't understand the new direction', 'It took me a long time to adjust'. Green flags: 'Requirements shifted mid-project and I reworked the solution', 'I learned new tech on the job', 'The change actually improved the outcome'.

q7.label: What is Technical Depth and what do interviewers look for?
q7.description: Explain the technical depth competency. What does technical depth really mean? What makes a strong answer?

metadata.answer: Technical Depth measures: Do you understand fundamentals deeply? Can you solve hard problems? Do you mentor others? Can you make informed tradeoffs? Interviewers look for: (1) Deep understanding - can you explain WHY, not just WHAT? (2) Complex problem-solving - did you break down hard problems? (3) Tradeoff thinking - did you consider multiple approaches? (4) Teaching others - did you mentor and level up the team? (5) Systems thinking - how did your solution fit the larger architecture? (6) Continuous learning - do you stay current? Red flags: 'I used this technology because it was trendy', 'The system is complex so I don't touch it', 'I can code but not design'. Green flags: 'I chose this tech stack because...', 'I refactored to support 10x growth', 'Junior engineers ask me to code review because they trust my judgment'.

behavioral_common_questions.label: Common Behavioral Interview Questions
behavioral_common_questions.description: Master the most frequently asked behavioral questions in interviews. Learn how to answer each effectively with real examples.

q1.label: Tell me about yourself (60 seconds)
q1.description: This is often the first question. How do you summarize yourself in 1 minute without rambling?

metadata.answer: I'm a backend engineer with 6 years of experience building scalable systems. I started at a small fintech startup where I built payment systems processing millions daily. Then I joined a mid-size e-commerce company where I led a team of 4 engineers and owned the order fulfillment platform - we handled peak seasons with 10x traffic spikes. Most recently, I've been focused on system design and mentoring junior engineers. I'm passionate about solving hard technical problems and building high-performing teams. I'm interested in this role because your architecture challenges align with my expertise, and I want to work on problems at this scale. My strengths are: technical depth, problem-solving under pressure, and helping teams grow.

q2.label: Why are you interested in this role?
q2.description: Show that you've done research and have specific reasons, not generic ones.

metadata.answer: I'm interested in this role for three specific reasons: First, your tech stack is exactly what I want to deepen - you're using distributed systems at scale, which is my passion area. Second, the role owns the API layer, and I've successfully led three API redesigns that improved performance by 40-60%. I'm excited to apply that here. Third, your company is solving a real problem in the logistics space - I've followed your product and the 10x improvement in delivery tracking is impressive. I think I can contribute to that mission. Also, the team size and structure appeal to me - I've learned that I do my best work in mid-sized teams (4-8 engineers) with clear ownership.

q3.label: Why are you interested in our company?
q3.description: Research the company. Show genuine interest, not just a paycheck.

metadata.answer: I've followed your company because of three things: (1) Your recent expansion into Asia - I'm interested in how you're localizing your platform and handling different infrastructure constraints. That's a hard problem. (2) Your engineering blog posts on real-time infrastructure - they're thoughtful and show a culture of sharing knowledge. (3) Your market position - you're the clear leader in this space, which means the technical problems are harder and the team is stronger. I want to work with people who challenge me. I also did some research on your culture - you have a high retention rate (87% over 3 years), which suggests people actually like working here. That matters to me.

q4.label: What's your biggest mistake or failure?
q4.description: Use STAR format. Own the failure. Show what you learned. Don't pick something trivial.

metadata.answer: My biggest mistake was underestimating complexity in a database migration. I estimated 3 weeks, but ended up taking 8 weeks. I set aggressive deadlines because I was overconfident in my technical abilities. The migration had cascading dependencies I didn't account for - it affected 5 different services. What made it worse: I didn't communicate early that we were falling behind. By the time I escalated, stakeholders were frustrated. What I did: I took full ownership - told the team it was my miscommunication and misjudgment. I broke down remaining work into daily milestones and reported status every morning. I paired with each team lead to unblock them. We shipped eventually, but learned hard lessons. What changed: Now I add 50% buffer to estimates, especially for cross-team work. I over-communicate timelines. I break big migrations into smaller phases. That experience actually made me better at planning. I now coach junior engineers on realistic estimation.

q5.label: Tell me about a conflict with a manager or peer
q5.description: Show maturity. Listen to their perspective. Find win-win. No blame.

metadata.answer: I had a disagreement with my manager about technical direction. I wanted to refactor a legacy service; she wanted to keep it as-is to ship features faster. I initially felt like she didn't understand the technical debt accumulating - replication lag was 10 seconds, which would become critical. Instead of pushing harder, I asked to understand her constraints. Turns out, the roadmap had aggressive feature commitments and refactoring would delay everything. So I proposed a compromise: phase the refactor over 3 sprints while shipping features. First sprint: optimize queries (low risk, high gain). Second sprint: refactor data model. Third sprint: stress test. She liked this because it reduced risk and didn't block features. We did it, and it actually gave us more breathing room for features. What I learned: my manager wasn't ignoring tech debt - she was balancing competing priorities I didn't fully see. Now I ask more questions before pushing back.

q6.label: Tell me about a project you're proud of
q6.description: Pick something with clear impact. Show your contribution. Use specific metrics.

metadata.answer: I'm really proud of the mobile API redesign I led. Context: Our mobile app was slow - API response times were 800ms-2s. We lost users because it felt sluggish. I owned the redesign. What I did: (1) Profiled the existing API - found N+1 queries, over-fetching data. (2) Redesigned with mobile in mind - compressed payloads 70%, batched requests. (3) Added caching - Redis for frequently accessed data. (4) Implemented pagination properly - 10x less data per request. (5) Built monitoring so we caught regressions. Results: API response times dropped to 100-200ms. Mobile app load time went from 4 seconds to 1 second. User retention in first week improved by 15%. App store rating improved from 3.8 to 4.6. What made me proud: it wasn't just technical - I worked with product to understand user pain points. I worked with QA to test edge cases. I mentored a junior engineer through the whole project. By the end, he could own future optimizations.

q7.label: Describe a time you handled ambiguity
q7.description: Show how you navigate unclear situations. Ask questions. Gather information. Make decisions.

metadata.answer: We were asked to 'improve checkout experience' with no specific brief. Ambiguous. I could've built random features and hoped. Instead: (1) I talked to 10 users - watched them checkout, asked what frustrated them. (2) I analyzed data - found 40% cart abandonment at payment step, 15% at shipping step. (3) I talked to support - they confirmed payment failures and shipping confusion as top issues. (4) I proposed: (a) simplify payment flow - reduce steps from 5 to 3, (b) show shipping cost upfront. (5) We built and shipped in 2 weeks. Results: Cart abandonment dropped to 28%. Conversion improved 12%. Revenue per user up 8%. What I learned: ambiguity isn't bad - it's an opportunity. Instead of waiting for perfect specs, ask questions, gather data, make small bets, iterate. That's how you work in startups.

q8.label: Tell me about working with a difficult person
q8.description: Don't badmouth them. Show your maturity and problem-solving.

metadata.answer: I worked with an engineer who was very opinionated and hard to collaborate with. He would dismiss ideas in code review, sometimes rudely. It created tension. At first, I avoided pairing with him. But we had to work together on a critical service. Instead of resenting it, I decided to understand him. I asked him to coffee and listened. Turned out: he cared deeply about code quality because a poorly-written system once caused production issues. His harsh feedback wasn't personal - it was perfectionism. Once I understood his motivation, I responded differently. In code reviews, I asked him questions: 'What concerns do you have about this approach?' instead of defending. He opened up - gave me really good feedback. We started pairing weekly. By the end, he became my strongest collaborator. We shipped something neither of us could have alone. What I learned: 'difficult' people are usually difficult for a reason. If you understand the reason, you can work with them. Also, being the first one to be vulnerable and understanding goes a long way.

q9.label: Tell me about giving feedback to someone senior
q9.description: Show courage and diplomacy. Clear communication. Focus on outcomes.

metadata.answer: I needed to give feedback to my VP about a decision. He decided to use a technology that I knew had adoption issues in our use case. I was nervous - he's senior, I'm an IC engineer. But I spoke up. I prepared: gathered data, didn't make it personal. I said: 'I have concerns about the tech choice - not because it's bad, but because it's a poor fit for our constraints. Can I share what I found?' He said yes. I showed: (1) Our scale requirements - technology X doesn't handle well, (2) Competition is using Y which scales better, (3) Implementation timeline - X has a steep learning curve, Y we have in-house expertise. He listened. He didn't dismiss me. We discussed. He decided to do a POC first instead of full commitment. POC showed my concerns were valid. We switched direction. Later, he thanked me for speaking up. What I learned: senior people actually want feedback if it's data-driven and respectful. They want people who think, not just execute. The key is: be respectful, back up your concerns with evidence, assume good intent.

q10.label: Why are you leaving your current role?

metadata.answer: I've learned a lot at my current company - I built systems handling 10x growth, mentored 3 engineers, and solved hard infrastructure problems. But I feel like I've maxed out what I can learn there. The company is stable, which is good, but the technical challenges have become routine. I want to work on harder problems - distributed systems at scale, handling 100x growth scenarios. Also, my growth path is limited - the senior engineer role is fully staffed. I don't want to wait 2 years for a manager role when I can jump into bigger technical challenges now. I'm looking for a company that: (1) has harder technical problems, (2) is in growth phase so I can impact scale decisions, (3) has senior engineers I can learn from. Your company fits all three. I'm excited about the possibility.

q12.label: What questions do you have for us?

metadata.answer: I have three questions: (1) 'I saw your company migrated from monolith to microservices last year. How is that going? (2) 'What does success look like for this role in the first year?' - This shows I care about impact and clear expectations. (3) 'How do you handle on-call and prevent burnout? What's the culture around it?' - This shows I care about sustainability and team health. Also: 'What's the biggest challenge your team is facing right now?' - Open-ended, lets them share what matters. Good questions show you're serious, you've researched, and you care about fit both ways.

behavioral_culture_fit.label: Culture Fit Assessment - Is This Company Right for You?
behavioral_culture_fit.description: Learn how to assess company culture during interviews. What are green flags and red flags? How to ask the right questions? Is this team one you want to join?

metadata.tags: behavioral, culture-fit, company-assessment, red-flags, green-flags, growth-mindset

q1.label: What are green flags that indicate a healthy culture?
q1.description: What should you look for that signals a company you want to join?

metadata.answer: Green flags for healthy culture: (1) Growth mindset - people talk about 'learning from failures', not blame culture. (2) Psychological safety - people ask questions without fear. Engineers disagree respectfully in meetings. (3) Transparency - company shares goals, metrics, challenges. You understand why decisions are made. (4) Mentorship - senior people spend time helping junior engineers grow. (5) Autonomy - teams own their decisions, not micromanaged. (6) Work-life balance signals - people leave at reasonable hours, take vacations. (7) Diversity in hiring and leadership - not all same demographic or background. (8) Staying power - people stay 3+ years (look on LinkedIn). (9) Engineers at senior levels - not just managers who came from sales. (10) Continuous learning budget - books, conferences, courses encouraged. (11) Interesting problems - not just CRUD apps, but real scale/complexity. (12) Humility - leaders admit when they're wrong. Watch for these in interviews and conversations.

q2.label: What are red flags that warn you to be cautious?
q2.description: What warning signs suggest culture problems?

metadata.answer: Red flags for unhealthy culture: (1) Blame culture - people blame 'the other team' or 'management'. (2) Hero culture - 'We need people to work 80-hour weeks'. (3) Secrecy - can't explain why decisions are made. (4) No mentorship - junior engineers left to figure things out. (5) Micromanagement - constant check-ins, no autonomy. (6) Politics - advancement based on relationships, not merit. (7) High turnover - people quit after 1-2 years. (8) Burnout signals - interviewers look tired, say 'it's always busy'. (9) Lack of diversity - same type of person everywhere. (10) Technical debt ignored - 'We're too busy for refactoring'. (11) No women/minorities in leadership. (12) Interviewer can't articulate company goals. (13) Questions only about hours/productivity, not growth. (14) 'We don't have time for testing' or 'just ship it'. Trust your gut - if something feels off, it probably is.

q3.label: How to evaluate team dynamics during interviews?
q3.description: What can you observe about how teams actually work together?

metadata.answer: Evaluate team dynamics by: (1) Watch how people interact - are they respectful, do they interrupt? (2) Ask 'Tell me about a disagreement on your team. How did you resolve it?' - Tells you if conflict is healthy. (3) Notice energy levels - are people engaged or checked out? (4) Ask 'What's something you learned from someone junior to you?' - Shows mentorship culture. (5) Observe communication - are people direct or political? (6) Ask 'How do you handle mistakes?' - Blame vs. learning? (7) Notice diversity - do you see different perspectives? (8) Ask 'What would you change about this team?' - Honesty or company lines? (9) Watch meeting interactions - do people contribute equally or is it top-down? (10) Ask about failure - 'Tell me about a project that failed.' Do they own it or blame? (11) Notice who's in the room - is there senior/junior mix or just senior engineers? (12) Ask 'How do you prevent burnout?' - Real answer or platitude? Pay attention to body language, tone, specificity of answers.

q4.label: How to assess learning and growth opportunities?
q4.description: Will this role help you grow your skills and career?

metadata.answer: Assess growth potential by: (1) Ask 'What's your growth path?' - Can people become senior/lead? Or is it a dead-end? (2) Ask 'Who's the most junior person who became senior?' - Shows growth happens. (3) Ask about mentorship - 'How do senior engineers help you grow?' (4) Ask about technical challenges - 'What's the hardest technical problem you've solved here?' (5) Ask about learning budget - Are conferences/courses paid for? Time allocated? (6) Ask 'What skills do you wish you had learned here?' - Tells you what's missing. (7) Ask about code review culture - 'How do people learn from each other?' (8) Ask about rotation - Can people try new areas? (9) Ask about projects - Will you work on interesting problems or just maintenance? (10) Ask about autonomy - 'Can you make architectural decisions or does everything need approval?' (11) Notice if senior people still code - or do they become managers/politics? (12) Ask 'What would this role look like in 2 years? In 5 years?' Red flag: If they can't articulate growth path or say 'you'll have the same job', avoid it.

q5.label: How to evaluate work-life balance realistically?
q5.description: Will you have time for life outside work or be sacrificed for the company?

metadata.answer: Evaluate work-life balance by: (1) Ask 'What time do you typically leave?' - Watch for hesitation or 'it depends'. (2) Ask 'Do people take vacations?' - Are they checked out or replying to emails? (3) Ask 'What's your on-call schedule?' - Every week? Every 6 months? (4) Ask 'How many people are on-call?' - 1 person = disaster. (5) Ask 'What happens if you're sick?' - Do people cover or do you work sick? (6) Notice energy of interviewers - do they look rested or burned out? (7) Ask 'Do you work weekends?' - Ever? Frequently? (8) Ask about crunch - 'Do you have busy/slow seasons?' How long does crunch last? (9) Ask 'What's the latest you've been up because of work?' - Their response tells you culture. (10) Ask 'Can you work remotely?' - Even if you don't want to, it's flexibility. (11) Ask about parental leave, sick leave - is it generous? (12) Look at LinkedIn - do people have hobbies/outside interests or is work their identity? Red flag: 'Everyone here works hard' or 'You have to be passionate enough to do weekends'. Good sign: People mention their families, hobbies, travel.

q6.label: What is exit velocity and why does it matter?
q6.description: How long do people stay? Is turnover a warning sign?

metadata.answer: Exit velocity is: How long people typically stay at the company before leaving. Why it matters: If people leave after 1-2 years, something's wrong - culture, compensation, growth, burnout. If people stay 4+ years, it's usually a healthy place. How to assess: (1) Look on LinkedIn - search for '[Company Name] - Past Employees'. See how long they stayed. (2) Calculate average tenure - if most left after 1.5 years, red flag. (3) Ask your interviewer - 'How long have you been here?' If they're senior and been there 2+ years, good sign. (4) Ask directly - 'What's the average tenure for engineers?' (5) Research Glassdoor reviews - what do departing employees say? (6) Ask 'Why do people leave?' - See if they're honest or defensive. (7) Ask 'How often do you lose people?' - If they say 'not often' but hiring constantly, something's up. Green flag: People stay 3+ years, leave because they got promoted or got specific opportunity, not because they're escaping. Red flag: High turnover, people leave after 1 year, reviews mention burnout or politics. Exit velocity is one of the strongest signals of culture health because people vote with their feet.

behavioral_negotiation.label: Offer Negotiation Basics
behavioral_negotiation.description: Master offer negotiation. Understand market rates, negotiation strategy, and how to get fair compensation without burning bridges.

metadata.tags: behavioral, negotiation, compensation, salary, offer, equity

q1.label: What's the market rate for your role and level?
q1.description: How do you know if an offer is fair? What should you research?

metadata.answer: Research market rate by: (1) Levels.fyi - crowdsourced salary data by company, role, level. Search 'Senior Engineer Google' and see distributions. (2) Blind - anonymous discussions by current employees about compensation. (3) LinkedIn Salary - filter by company, role, location. (4) Glassdoor - average salary, though sometimes outdated. (5) PayScale - role-based salary research. (6) Ask mentors - 'What's typical for this role at this level in this city?' (7) Ask recruiters - they know market rates. (8) Consider: location matters - SF/NYC premium 30-50% over midwest. (9) Consider: company stage - startup equity vs FAANG cash. (10) Consider: experience - add 10-15% per year if you have relevant experience. (11) Consider: negotiating power - do you have competing offers? (12) Typical breakdown: 60-70% base salary, 20-30% equity/bonus, 10% benefits. Example: Senior Engineer in SF, 8 years experience: base $180-220K, equity $100-150K/year over 4 years, bonus 15-20%. Don't accept first offer - first offer is typically 10-20% below market for negotiation room.

q2.label: What's the negotiation strategy and timeline?
q2.description: How to negotiate effectively without losing the offer?

metadata.answer: Negotiation strategy: (1) Don't accept immediately - say 'Thank you, I'm excited. Can I review the details and get back to you in 2 days?' (2) Get offer in writing - always. Email confirmation. (3) Research market rate before responding - gather data. (4) Prepare your counter - have specific numbers ready. (5) Lead with enthusiasm - 'I'm excited about the role, here's what I'd need...' not 'This is too low'. (6) Be specific - counter with exact numbers, not ranges. Example: 'I'd like to counter with $210K base' not 'I want more'. (7) Justify - 'Based on market data and my experience, $210K is standard for this role.' (8) Negotiate holistically - don't just ask for base. Ask about: signing bonus, equity cliff (when you earn equity), bonus structure, stock refresh, options, benefits, title, remote work, vacation, professional development budget. (9) Have your BATNA ready - Best Alternative to Negotiated Agreement - what's your backup? Another offer? Staying in current job? (10) Timeline - most companies give 1-2 weeks to decide. Use that time. (11) Stay professional - never be aggressive or demanding. Always assume good faith. (12) Know when to stop - after 2-3 rounds of back-and-forth, accept or walk. Pushing too hard can lose offer. (13) Get final offer in writing - don't negotiate verbally.

q3.label: How to counter-offer effectively?
q3.description: What's a good counter-offer? How to present it?

metadata.answer: Counter-offer example: Offer: $180K base, $100K equity (25K/year), 15% bonus. Counter: 'I appreciate the offer. I'm very excited about the role. Based on market research (Levels.fyi), the range for this role is $200-220K base. Given my 8 years of experience and background in distributed systems (relevant to your needs), I'd like to counter with: Base: $210K, Equity: $130K (32.5K/year), Bonus: 20%, Signing bonus: $30K. This brings my comp to market level while acknowledging this is a strong offer.' Then: (1) Wait for their response. (2) They might counter back - 'We can do $200K base, $130K equity, keep 15% bonus.' (3) You decide - is that acceptable? Or counter again? (4) Example second counter: 'I appreciate the move on base and equity. Can we do $205K base and bump signing bonus to $50K?' (5) Key: each counter should be smaller - don't re-open old asks. (6) Know your walk-away number - if they can't meet minimum, what do you do? (7) Typical outcome: You get 50-70% of what you asked for. If you ask for $230K and counter at $220K, you might end at $205K. That's a win. Remember: Negotiation is normal. Companies expect it. They budget for it. If you don't negotiate, you're leaving money on the table.

q4.label: What is equity and how to evaluate it?
q4.description: Understanding stocks, RSUs, stock options in your offer

metadata.answer: Types of equity: (1) Stock Options (startups) - right to buy stock at strike price. Example: 0.5% of company with $1 strike. If company sells for $1B, your equity worth $5M. Risk: If company fails, worth $0. Vesting: typically 4 years with 1-year cliff. (2) RSUs - Restricted Stock Units (public companies). Company gives you $100K worth of stock. Vests monthly/quarterly over 4 years. If stock goes up, you win. If it tanks, you lose. (3) Bonus - annual bonus, typically 15-20%. (4) Signing Bonus - one-time payment when you join. How to evaluate: (1) Ask: When is vesting cliff? (month where you own 25% typically) (2) Ask: What's the vesting schedule? (Usually 25% at 1 year, then monthly over remaining 3 years) (3) Ask: What happens to unvested equity if I leave? (Usually you lose it. Bad.) (4) Ask: Is there a refresh? (After vesting, do you get new equity?) (5) For startups: Ask valuation. If $100M valuation and $50K worth of equity offered - that's 0.05% (rough). (6) Ask about dilution. Future funding rounds dilute your %. (7) Calculate year-1 vesting - if 1M shares at $1 strike, and 25% vests in year 1, you own 250K shares. (8) For RSUs: If $100K RSUs over 4 years, that's $25K/year = about $2K/month income (minus taxes). (9) Don't count on equity getting rich. Count on base salary living. Equity is upside. Rule of thumb: Startup equity valuable only if company exits or IPOs. FAANG RSUs are basically stable income.

q5.label: What about signing bonus and other benefits?
q5.description: Negotiating beyond just base and equity

metadata.answer: Other negotiable items: (1) Signing Bonus - helps offset leaving bonus from previous job. $50-100K typical for senior roles. Negotiate if moving costs are high. (2) Stock Refresh - after your initial equity vests, do you get new equity? Important for long-term. (3) Bonus Structure - is it 15% guaranteed or at-risk? (4) Professional Development Budget - $2K-5K/year for courses, conferences. (5) Vacation - more vacation > more money in some cases. (6) Remote Work - remote is worth money (no relocation, lower COL). (7) Flexible Hours - do you need flexibility? (8) Health Insurance - family plan or individual? (9) 401k Match - do they match contributions? (10) Parental Leave - paid leave is significant. (11) Stock Grants Refresh - 'New hire grants + annual refresh' is better than one-time grant. (12) Title - negotiate title if it affects next job. (13) Work from anywhere - travel policy, can work from different cities? Negotiate strategy: Prioritize your needs. If you have kids, parental leave > extra $10K. If you're remote-focused, flexible work > extra base. Create a scorecard: Base salary (weight: 40%), Equity (weight: 30%), Signing Bonus (weight: 10%), Benefits (weight: 10%), Flexibility (weight: 10%). Evaluate total package holistically, not just base number.

q6.label: What's a BATNA and why does it matter?
q6.description: Know your alternatives to make strong negotiating decisions

metadata.answer: BATNA = Best Alternative to Negotiated Agreement. Why it matters: It's your walk-away point. If negotiation doesn't work, what do you do? Examples: (1) BATNA = another offer from Google for $220K. Your negotiating power: High. Push harder for $220K+ (2) BATNA = stay in current job. Your negotiating power: Medium. You have runway, not desperate. (3) BATNA = no other offers, been unemployed 6 months. Your negotiating power: Low. Less room to push. (4) BATNA = can take 6 months sabbatical before next job. Your negotiating power: High. You're not desperate. How to use BATNA: (1) Know it before negotiating. (2) Don't mention it unless asked. (3) If they ask 'Do you have other offers?', you can say 'I do' if true, or 'I have options' if you have a BATNA. (4) Let your BATNA influence your walk-away number. If BATNA is stay in current job at $180K, your walk-away should be >= $180K. (5) Reality: Once you have an offer, your leverage is strongest. After you accept, leverage disappears. Use it while you have it. (6) Red flag: If you have no BATNA and they know it, they have leverage. (7) Strategy: Don't go into negotiation without a BATNA or a reason to be confident. Generate optionality - apply to multiple companies so you have choices.

metadata.answer: Multiple offers strategy: (1) Time your responses - don't accept first offer immediately. Tell them you need 2 weeks to consider. (2) Tell other companies you have offers - 'I have an offer from X, decision due in 2 weeks.' They often accelerate. (3) Get written offers from all before deciding. (4) Don't play games - don't lie about offers. Do say 'I'm in late stages with other companies.' (5) When you have multiple offers, compare holistically, not just base. Startup $180K + 1% equity vs Google $200K + $120K RSUs - very different. (6) Example: Offer A: Startup, $180K + equity, interesting problem. Offer B: Google, $200K + $120K RSUs, stable. Consider: Risk tolerance, growth, compensation, culture, location. (7) Use multiple offers to negotiate - 'I have offer from Google for $200K. Can you match?' (8) Don't wait too long - once you have 2-3 offers and have decided, choose. Companies get annoyed if you drag it out. (9) Once you accept, stick to it - don't keep negotiating or keep talking to other companies. That's bridge-burning. (10) Negotiate in good faith. Remember: the recruiter/hiring manager will remember you. You might interview with them again in 5 years. Stay professional.

q8.label: What not to do during negotiation?

metadata.answer: Mistakes to avoid: (1) Negotiating via email for major things - too easy to miscommunicate. Use phone or in-person. (2) Being aggressive - 'This offer is insulting' or 'I deserve more' - sounds entitled. (3) Sharing other offers publicly - keeps it confidential. (4) Moving goalposts - don't agree to $210K then ask for $230K later. (5) Slow-playing - don't take 3 weeks to think if you only need 2. They get impatient. (6) Multiple rapid counter-offers - negotiate once or twice, then decide. (7) Being greedy - if you push too hard, they might pull offer. (8) Negotiating things they can't control - 'Can you change the CEO?' Negotiate only what's flexible. (9) Comparing to other employees - 'So-and-so got more money.' They won't like being compared. (10) Asking for benefits you don't need - focus on what matters to you. (11) Lying - never lie about other offers, salary, experience. (12) Changing your mind - once you accept, accept. (13) Asking your current manager for advice - they might want to keep you and poison negotiations. (14) Trusting only verbal agreements - always get offers in writing. Red flags: If they say 'Don't negotiate, just accept' or 'This is final, take it or leave it' early - it might be a power-tripping company. Good companies expect negotiation.

behavioral_red_flags.label: Red Flags in Interview Process
behavioral_red_flags.description: Learn what warning signs indicate a problematic company or interview process. Trust your instincts. Not all jobs are worth taking.

q1.label: What does a disorganized interview process indicate?
q1.description: Bad interviews reveal bad operations

metadata.answer: Disorganized interview process red flags: (1) No feedback between rounds - you interview, hear nothing for 2 weeks. Then suddenly they want final round. (2) Interviewers unprepared - they don't know your resume, ask repeated questions, seem confused. (3) Unclear interview schedule - 'We'll call you sometime next week' instead of 'Tuesday 2pm'. (4) Rescheduling constantly - shows they don't have their act together. (5) Different people give contradictory information - one says remote, another says office 5 days. (6) No one owns the process - nobody seems responsible for keeping things moving. (7) Ghosting - apply, no response for weeks, then suddenly urgent. (8) Technical issues - Zoom call fails, they don't have meeting setup ready. (9) Asking you to solve problems on the spot without notice - 'Can you code for us right now?' (10) Nobody explains the process - you don't know how many rounds, what's next, when you'll hear. (11) Ad-hoc hiring - feels like they're making it up as they go. (12) What it means: If they're disorganized about hiring (which is important), what about operations? Disorganized process often reflects disorganized company. Exception: Small startups might be chaotic but in a fun way. But most companies that get hiring right also get operations right. Trust your gut - if you feel confused during interviewing, they probably confuse employees too.

q2.label: What does vague role description tell you?
q2.description: Unclear expectations often mean unclear company

metadata.answer: Vague role description red flags: (1) Job description is generic - could apply to any job. (2) Responsibilities undefined - 'Help with backend stuff' vs 'Own the payment service'. (3) No clear owner - 'You'll work with the team on infrastructure' but no specific owner. (4) Team size unclear - 'You'll manage the backend' but unclear if it's 2 people or 10. (5) Reporting unclear - 'Reports to someone in engineering' but who exactly? (6) Success metrics undefined - How do you know you're doing well? (7) Tech stack unclear - what are you actually building on? (8) Scope keeps changing - they describe role one way, then say 'We also need you to do X, Y, Z'. (9) Nobody can articulate the role - each interviewer describes it differently. (10) Interviewers seem unsure - they hesitate or change answers. What it means: Vague role often means: (a) They haven't thought through what they need. (b) Role will be whatever they need (scope creep). (c) Unclear expectations = unclear evaluation = unfair review. (d) You're a firefighter, not a person with a career path. What to do: Push back. Ask 'What does success look like in 30 days? 6 months? 1 year?' If they can't answer, be wary. Ask 'What are the top 3 problems you want me to solve?' If they struggle, the role is unclear.

q3.label: What's a warning sign about career growth?
q3.description: Dead-end jobs hurt your future

metadata.answer: Career growth red flags: (1) No path to senior - 'You'll be an IC for life' or 'We don't have senior roles here'. (2) Can't point to promotions - 'We don't really do promotions, just raises'. (3) No examples of growth - when you ask 'Who advanced here?', they struggle to name people. (4) Role feels like a capstone - 'This is the final level you can reach in this company'. (5) Skills won't transfer - 'You'll learn this proprietary system but it's not used elsewhere'. (6) No mentorship - you'll be isolated, learning nothing from others. (7) Same work for years - 'People have been doing this exact job for 5+ years'. (8) No new challenges - 'The codebase is stable, you'll just maintain it'. (9) No interesting problems - boring tech, boring work. (10) Title doesn't advance - you'll be 'Engineer' in 5 years, no growth. What it means: If you can't grow here, it's just a paycheck. Your resume won't improve. Your skills won't advance. In 5 years, you'll be stuck. Rule: Always ask 'What happened to the person who was in this role before? Where are they now?' If they were promoted, growth is possible. If they left, ask why.

q4.label: What indicates no technical mentorship?
q4.description: Senior engineers don't mean good mentorship

metadata.answer: No mentorship red flags: (1) No senior engineers on the team - all intermediate/junior. (2) Senior people don't code - they're in politics/management. (3) Senior people don't have time to help - 'They're too busy with meetings'. (4) No code review culture - 'We don't have time for reviews, just commit'. (5) No pairing/mentoring - everyone works independently. (6) New people struggle alone - 'Figure it out' mentality. (7) Turnover of junior people - they leave after 1 year because they didn't learn. (8) Questions not welcomed - 'Just read the code' instead of 'Let me explain'. (9) Mistakes criticized harshly - 'That was dumb' instead of 'Let's improve this'. (10) No knowledge sharing - each person siloed, no tech talks. (11) Onboarding is self-service - no assigned mentor or buddy. (12) Learning isn't valued - 'We don't have time for training'. What it means: Without mentorship, you'll: (1) Learn slower, (2) Make more mistakes, (3) Not level up your skills, (4) Get frustrated and leave, (5) Hurt your career growth. At junior/mid level, the mentor matters more than salary. Ask: 'Who would be my mentor?' 'Can I spend time pairing with senior engineers?' If answers are unclear, be wary. Good sign: They assign you a mentor in first week.

q5.label: What does high turnover reveal?
q5.description: People vote with their feet

metadata.answer: High turnover red flags: (1) LinkedIn - many people with 1-2 year tenures. (2) They're always hiring - 'We're hiring 5 engineers this quarter' constantly. (3) Ask team members directly - 'How many people left your team this year?' If more than 1-2, that's high. (4) Reasons unclear - when you ask why people leave, they give vague answers. (5) Recent departures - 'Three people left last month', that's a pattern. (6) Onboarding talks about retention - 'We're working on keeping people' suggests it's a known problem. (7) Glassdoor reviews mention turnover - 'People don't stay long' or 'High burnout'. (8) Manager changed multiple times - 'We're on third manager this year' means your manager might leave too. (9) Can't point to long-term people - nobody's been there 5+ years. (10) Blaming employees - 'People aren't committed' (actually, company is the problem). What it means: High turnover signals: (a) Bad culture (b) Bad compensation (c) Bad management (d) Bad work-life balance (e) Unclear growth. People don't leave good jobs. Ask: 'What's the average tenure?' If it's under 3 years, something's wrong. Red flag: 'We just got new leadership and they're making changes' - transition periods are risky. You might be caught in change you didn't sign up for.

q6.label: What's a warning sign about company politics?
q6.description: Politics kill good engineers

metadata.answer: Politics red flags: (1) Blame culture - 'Backend team messed up the launch' or 'Frontend doesn't know how to design'. (2) Silos - teams don't talk, information is gatekept. (3) Credit-stealing - people brag about work they didn't do. (4) Meetings about meetings - endless standups/syncs instead of working. (5) Advancement based on relationships - 'You need to know the right people to get promoted'. (6) CYA culture - people send excessive emails to protect themselves, not to communicate. (7) Micromanagement - managers nitpick code instead of trusting work. (8) Turf wars - 'That's not our team's problem' mentality. (9) Undefined ownership - 'Everyone owns this so nobody owns this'. (10) Gossip culture - interviewers mention colleagues negatively or with cynicism. (11) Politics mentioned explicitly - 'There's a lot of politics here'. (12) Defensive communication - people careful about what they say around leadership. What it means: In politics-heavy companies: (a) Energy goes to politics, not innovation. (b) Good people leave, mediocre survivors. (c) Your code doesn't matter, your relationships do. (d) You'll spend energy managing up instead of shipping. (e) Burnout is hidden but real. Ask: 'Tell me about conflict on the team. How is it resolved?' Watch their answer - do they blame others or own problems? Say: 'Tell me about someone who got promoted. What did they do well?' If the answer is 'They're close with the CEO', that's politics. If it's 'They shipped X feature', that's meritocracy.

q7.label: What indicates unhealthy blame culture?
q7.description: Blame culture kills learning and psychology safety

metadata.answer: Blame culture red flags: (1) Production goes down and people point fingers instead of fixing. (2) Mistakes are career-ending - 'One engineer made a mistake and we fired them'. (3) Blamestorming instead of debugging - 'Whose fault was this?' not 'How do we prevent it?' (4) No psychological safety - people don't speak up about problems. (5) Hiding failures - when something breaks, people cover it up instead of escalating. (6) Harsh language - 'That was stupid', 'That's broken', calling out individuals. (7) Post-mortems are not blameless - 'We need to find who to blame', not 'What can we improve?' (8) People don't try new things - too risky if failure means blame. (9) Senior people are never blamed - 'They made the right call, implementer messed up'. (10) Questions are seen as challenges - 'Why are you questioning that?' instead of 'Great question'. (11) Interviewers seem afraid - they watch their words carefully. (12) Talk about former employees negatively - 'That guy was terrible', real reason might be he asked too many questions. What it means: In blame culture: (a) People don't admit mistakes early, problems fester. (b) Innovation stops because people fear failure. (c) Talented people leave, only sycophants remain. (d) Your mental health suffers - constant fear. (e) You won't grow because you'll avoid risks. Good culture: Mistakes are learning opportunities. Compare questions about blame with questions about learning. Ask: 'Can you tell me about a recent failure your team had? How did you respond?' Good answer: 'We had a performance issue, post-mortem showed X, we fixed Y, now we prevent Z.' Bad answer: 'Engineer deployed bad code' (blaming). 'We're reviewing who made the mistake' (politics).

q8.label: What's a red flag if they don't ask about you?

metadata.answer: Red flag: Interviewers only talk about themselves/company, never ask about you. Specific signs: (1) Interview is one-way - they ask questions, you answer, they don't ask about your goals. (2) They describe the role but don't ask what interests you. (3) They sell the role aggressively - like you need convincing to want to work there. (4) Nobody asks about your career goals - 'Where do you want to be in 5 years?' (5) Nobody asks about your needs - 'What matters to you in a role?' (6) Nobody asks about your experience - 'Tell us about your background.' (7) Interviewer seems annoyed by your questions - 'We don't have time, just say yes or no'. (8) Nobody explains the role beyond job description. (9) They act like you're lucky to interview there - 'Most people would kill for this'. (10) Nobody asks 'Are you interested?' or 'Do you have concerns?' What it means: Selfish interviewers indicate: (a) Company doesn't care about fit, only filling slots. (b) They'll be demanding without understanding your needs. (c) Work-life balance won't be respected - your needs don't matter. (d) Feedback will be one-way - they tell you what to do. (e) You're a resource, not a person. Good interviews are two-way: They sell the role, you assess fit. You ask questions, they answer thoughtfully. They ask about you, genuinely. You get sense they want you AND want to make sure you want them. If interview feels like they don't care about you, pass.

q9.label: What's the red flag of pressure to decide immediately?
q9.description: Rushed decisions are often bad decisions

metadata.answer: Pressure to decide red flags: (1) 'We need an answer by EOD tomorrow'. (2) 'We have other candidates, you need to decide fast'. (3) 'This offer expires in 24 hours'. (4) 'Everyone decides within a week'. (5) 'If you don't say yes, we'll move on'. (6) Creating artificial scarcity - 'We only have one spot open'. (7) Getting emotional - 'We're really excited and need to know NOW'. (8) Guilt - 'We've spent so much time on you, we need a decision'. (9) FOMO - 'You won't get another chance like this'. (10) Pressuring without reason - offers should give 2 weeks minimum. Why it's a red flag: (a) Good companies are patient - they want the right person, not fast person. (b) Pressure indicates: They're desperate, or they test if you'll cave under pressure. (c) If they pressure you before hiring, they'll pressure you during employment. (d) It's manipulative - using scarcity and urgency to bypass your rational thinking. (e) Your gut instinct might be screaming 'no' but pressure makes you ignore it. What to do: (1) If pressured, say 'I appreciate the urgency. I need until [date] to decide. If that doesn't work for you, I understand.' (2) If they rescind over your reasonable timeline, they're not worth it. (3) Trust companies that give you time. (4) Get the offer in writing so you can think clearly. (5) Remember: This is your life decision, not their schedule decision. Pressure is a manipulation tactic. Good companies don't use it.

q10.label: What does contradictory feedback indicate?
q10.description: Misalignment in leadership or unclear standards

metadata.answer: Contradictory feedback red flags: (1) One interviewer says 'You'd own the architecture decisions', another says 'Management makes all decisions'. (2) One says 'Remote is fine', another says 'In office 5 days'. (3) One says 'We're not hiring more engineers', another says 'We're scaling fast'. (4) One says 'Flexible hours', another says 'We need presence 9-5'. (5) One interviewer says the role is easy, another says it's hard. (6) Leadership vs individual contributors give different stories. (7) Compensation details change - 'Equity vests immediately' then later 'Standard 4-year vest'. (8) Company goals change between interviews - 'We're focusing on X' then 'Actually Y'. (9) Responsibility unclear - 'You'll report to X' vs 'You'll report to Y'. (10) Team structure unclear - multiple versions of who you'd work with. What it means: (a) Company is disorganized - leadership hasn't aligned internally. (b) Expectations will be unclear - and confusion will be your fault. (c) Different departments give different answers because they don't communicate. (d) When you join, you'll be caught between different expectations. (e) If they haven't aligned on the role, they haven't aligned on you. What to do: (1) Document what each person says. (2) In final round, clarify with the most senior person - 'Here's what I heard, is this accurate?' (3) If contradictions persist, ask directly - 'I'm hearing different things. Can you clarify?' (4) If they can't clarify, that's the problem. (5) Get the offer details in writing - binding. Good sign: Leadership is aligned, each interviewer tells the same story, contradictions are explained. Bad sign: Each interviewer seems to work for a different company.

coding_patterns.label: Core Coding Patterns - LeetCode Medium Level
coding_patterns.description: Master the essential coding patterns for technical interviews. Recognize patterns in problems and apply the right approach. Focus on pattern recognition, not memorization.

q1.label: What are Two Pointers and when to use them?
q1.description: Explain the two pointers pattern and problems it solves

metadata.answer: Two Pointers Pattern: Use two pointers moving at different speeds/directions to solve problems. When to use: (1) Array is sorted - move pointers from ends toward middle, (2) Finding pairs/triplets in array, (3) Removing elements, (4) Partitioning arrays. Examples: Reverse string (pointers at ends moving inward), Two Sum II sorted array (left/right pointers), Container with Most Water (left/right moving inward based on height). Complexity: O(n) time, O(1) space. Common mistakes: Not handling duplicates, not understanding when to move each pointer, using when array is unsorted (won't work). Recognition: If problem asks about pairs, removals, or mentions sorted array - consider two pointers.
metadata.python:
def reverseString(s):
    left, right = 0, len(s) - 1
    while left < right:
        s[left], s[right] = s[right], s[left]
        left += 1
        right -= 1
    return s

q2.label: What is Sliding Window and how to apply it?
q2.description: Explain sliding window pattern for subarray/substring problems

metadata.answer: Sliding Window Pattern: Maintain a window of elements (left/right pointers) that slides through array/string. When to use: (1) Find max/min subarray/substring of size k, (2) Longest substring without repeating chars, (3) Max sum subarray, (4) Anagram problems. How it works: Expand window by moving right pointer, shrink by moving left pointer. Use hash map/set to track window contents. Complexity: O(n) time, O(k) space. Example: 'abcabcbb' - find longest substring without repeating. Use set to track chars in window. Right pointer adds chars, if duplicate found, left pointer removes chars until duplicate gone. Recognition: 'Longest/shortest substring/subarray' or 'Contains all' patterns often need sliding window.
metadata.python:
def maxSubArray(nums, k):
    window_sum = sum(nums[:k])
    max_sum = window_sum
    for i in range(k, len(nums)):
        window_sum += nums[i] - nums[i-k]
        max_sum = max(max_sum, window_sum)
    return max_sum

q3.label: When do you use Hash Map/Set patterns?
q3.description: Hash structures for fast lookup and counting

metadata.answer: Hash Map/Set Pattern: Use dictionaries/sets for O(1) lookups. When to use: (1) Counting elements (frequency map), (2) Finding duplicates, (3) Two sum problems (store complements), (4) Anagrams, (5) Valid parentheses, (6) Group anagrams. Hash Map: Key-value storage, track counts. Hash Set: Unique elements, fast membership check. Complexity: O(n) average, O(n²) worst case (collisions). Common usage: 'Given array, find if two numbers sum to target' - create map of complements as you iterate. Example: Two Sum - iterate array, check if (target - num) exists in map, add num to map. Recognition: If problem needs 'count', 'find duplicate', 'two elements that satisfy condition' - likely needs hash structure.

q4.label: How do Stack and Queue patterns work?
q4.description: LIFO and FIFO data structure patterns

metadata.answer: Stack (LIFO) Pattern: Last In First Out. Use when: (1) Parentheses matching, (2) Undo/redo, (3) DFS without recursion, (4) Expression evaluation, (5) Monotonic stack problems. Example: Valid parentheses - push opening, pop on closing, match types. Queue (FIFO) Pattern: First In First Out. Use when: (1) BFS traversal, (2) Task scheduling, (3) Level-order traversal, (4) Print order patterns. Implementation: Python list with append/pop (stack) or deque (queue for O(1) operations). Complexity: O(1) push/pop. Common mistakes: Using list for queue (O(n) on left), forgetting to check empty before pop. Recognition: 'Valid brackets', 'next greater element', 'daily temperatures' = likely stack. 'Level order', 'shortest path', 'BFS' = likely queue.

q5.label: When and how to use Heap (Priority Queue)?
q5.description: Heap pattern for finding min/max efficiently

metadata.answer: Heap Pattern: Binary tree where parent < children (min heap) or parent > children (max heap). Use when: (1) Find K largest/smallest elements, (2) Median in stream, (3) Merge K sorted lists, (4) Task scheduling by priority. Time complexity: O(log n) insert/remove, O(1) peek min/max. Python: heapq module (min heap by default). Max heap: use negative values. Example: K Largest Elements - use min heap of size K, if element > min in heap, pop min and push element. Recognition: 'Kth largest', 'median', 'merge sorted' often need heaps. Common pattern: Maintain heap of size K for K-largest problems. Don't create heap of all n elements - inefficient. Instead heap of k, time O(n log k).
metadata.python:
import heapq
def findKLargest(nums, k):
    return heapq.nlargest(k, nums)
    # Or using min-heap: heap = nums[:k]; heapq.heapify(heap)
    # for num in nums[k:]: 
    #     if num > heap[0]: heapreplace(heap, num)
    # return heap

q6.label: Binary Search pattern - when and how?
q6.description: Efficient searching in sorted data

metadata.answer: Binary Search Pattern: Divide and conquer in sorted array. Complexity: O(log n). When to use: (1) Sorted array, (2) Find target/closest value, (3) Answer binary search (can we do this in X time?), (4) Range finding. How it works: left=0, right=n-1. While left <= right: mid=(left+right)//2. If arr[mid]==target return mid. Else if arr[mid]<target, search right half (left=mid+1). Else search left half (right=mid-1). Variants: Find first occurrence (continue left even if found), find last (continue right). Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors, infinite loops from wrong mid calculation, not handling edge cases. Recognition: 'Sorted array' + 'find' usually suggests binary search. Can also use for 'answer search' - if problem asks 'minimum days to X' on unsorted input, use binary search on answer (days 1-365), check if each day satisfies condition.

q7.label: DFS/BFS patterns for trees and graphs

metadata.answer: DFS (Depth-First Search) Pattern: Go deep before wide. Use recursion (easier) or stack. When: (1) Tree traversal (pre/in/post-order), (2) Detect cycles, (3) Topological sort, (4) Connected components. Example: Preorder - visit node, recurse left, recurse right. BFS (Breadth-First Search) Pattern: Wide before deep. Use queue. When: (1) Level-order traversal, (2) Shortest path in unweighted graph, (3) Closest elements, (4) Connected components. Example: Level-order tree - queue initial node, dequeue and add children to queue. Both O(V+E) time where V=vertices, E=edges. DFS: O(h) space for recursion stack where h=height. BFS: O(w) space where w=width. Recognition: Tree/graph problems usually need DFS or BFS. If asking 'path exists', 'connected', 'distance' - likely need traversal.

q8.label: Dynamic Programming - when to recognize and apply?

metadata.answer: Dynamic Programming Pattern: Solve problem by breaking into overlapping subproblems, memoize results. When to use: (1) Optimal substructure - optimal solution uses optimal solutions of subproblems, (2) Overlapping subproblems - same subproblem solved multiple times. Recognition: 'Max/min ways to do X', 'number of ways', 'longest subsequence', 'can we achieve X' often need DP. Steps: (1) Define state: dp[i] = answer for problem of size i, (2) Find recurrence: dp[i] = f(dp[i-1], dp[i-2], ...), (3) Base cases: dp[0], dp[1], (4) Build bottom-up. Complexity: Usually O(n²) or O(n³) time, O(n) space (can optimize space). Example: Climbing stairs - dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] (reach step i from i-1 or i-2). Common mistakes: Wrong state definition, forgetting base cases, not recognizing overlapping subproblems. Ask interviewer: 'Am I on right track?' DP problems hard to code on spot, explain approach first.

coding_system_design.label: System Design + Coding Hybrids
coding_system_design.description: Implement system-level components in code. Combine design thinking with actual implementation. Real-world problems that require both architecture and coding.

metadata.tags: system-design, coding, implementation, cache, distributed, data-structures

q1.label: Implement LRU Cache
q1.description: Design and code a Least Recently Used cache

metadata.answer: LRU Cache: Fixed size cache, evict least recently used item when full. Design: Use hash map for O(1) get/put + doubly linked list for O(1) reordering. When access/set, move node to front (most recent). When full, remove tail (least recent). Implementation: dict + deque/linked list. Methods: get(key) O(1), put(key,value) O(1). Key insight: Hash map gives O(1) lookup, doubly linked list gives O(1) reordering. Single linked list won't work - need prev pointers to remove from middle. When interviewer asks: (1) Clarify capacity and size, (2) What happens on get - does it count as 'use'? Usually yes. (3) Discuss why doubly linked list needed. (4) Walk through example: LRU([1,2,3], cap=2) - put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1), put(3,3) - 2 evicted, cache=[1,3].

q2.label: Design Rate Limiter (Token Bucket)
q2.description: Implement rate limiting with token bucket algorithm

metadata.answer: Rate Limiter using Token Bucket: Allow N requests per second. How it works: Bucket has capacity C, refilled at rate R tokens/sec. Each request costs 1 token. If tokens available, allow and decrement. If not, reject. Implementation: Track last_refill_time, current_tokens. On each request: elapsed = now - last_refill_time, tokens += elapsed * rate, tokens = min(tokens, capacity). If tokens >= 1: allow (tokens -= 1), else deny. Complexity: O(1) per request. Code structure: class RateLimiter with init(capacity, rate), isAllowed() -> bool. Key insight: Don't need queue, just track timestamps and tokens. Edge cases: What if time goes backward? Clock skew? Usually assume monotonic time. Example: 3 req/sec, cap=10. Start: tokens=10. Request at t=0: allow (tokens=9). Request at t=0.2: allow (tokens=8). Request at t=1: tokens += 1*3 = 8+3=11, min(11,10)=10. Allow (tokens=9). If 5 requests at t=1.1, first 2 allowed, rest denied.

q3.label: Implement Consistent Hashing
q3.description: Distribute data across servers with minimal redistribution on server changes

metadata.answer: Consistent Hashing: Map keys and servers to circular hash space [0, 2^32). Key insight: On server add/remove, only O(n/m) keys redistribute (n=keys, m=servers), not all keys. How it works: (1) Hash each server to positions on circle, (2) Hash each key to position, (3) Find next server clockwise. Implementation: sorted dict/tree of server hashes. On get(key): find_server(hash(key)) = smallest server_hash >= key_hash (or wrap around). Virtual nodes: Each server gets K virtual nodes (replicas on circle) to balance load. Why: Without virtual nodes, one server might get much more keys. Example: 3 servers, 150 keys. Virtual nodes=5. Total 15 positions on circle. Keys distributed roughly evenly. Complexity: Binary search for O(log m) per key (where m=virtual nodes). Code: Use sorted dict/tree, need floor/ceiling operations. Add server: add K hashes, redistribute O(n/m) keys. Remove server: remove K hashes, redistribute O(n/m) keys.

q4.label: Design and Code URL Shortening Service
q4.description: Implement backend for URL shortening with actual code

metadata.answer: URL Shortener: Map long URLs to short codes. Design: hash map for O(1) lookup both directions. Encoding: base62 (0-9, a-z, A-Z) gives short codes. Implementation: (1) Global counter starting at some number, (2) encode(counter) -> base62 string, (3) store longUrl[short_code] = long_url, (4) store urlToCode[long_url] = short_code. Methods: encode(long_url) - if exists return code, else counter++, encode to base62, store, return. decode(code) - return longUrl[code]. Base62 conversion: repeatedly divide by 62, take remainder as digit. Example: 1 -> '1', 62 -> '10', 3844 -> '100'. Code: def encode(num): chars='0..9a..zA..Z', result='', while num: result=chars[num%62]+result, num//=62. Handling collisions: Use counter per server (distributed), or use hash of URL + timestamp. Discuss with interviewer: counter-based deterministic vs hash-based, distributed ID generation (Snowflake), TTL for URLs.

q5.label: Implement Distributed Counter
q5.description: Design counter that handles concurrent increments across servers

metadata.answer: Distributed Counter: Multiple servers incrementing shared counter concurrently. Naive approach: Single DB counter, O(1) but contention. Better: Local bucket per server. How: Each server has local_counter. On increment: local_counter++. On get_total: sum all server local_counters. Problem: Reads not real-time accurate. Solution: Add batching - when local_counter reaches batch_size, flush to global counter. This reduces DB writes from O(n requests) to O(n/batch_size). Tradeoff: Eventual consistency. Implementation: class Counter with local_counter, batch_size, global_counter_key. increment(): local++, if local==batch_size, atomically add to global, reset local. total(): read global counter (might be stale by up to batch_size). If need stronger consistency, add lock (but then back to contention). Discuss: batch_size tradeoff - larger = less writes but more stale, smaller = more accurate but more contention. Real world: Google AdWords uses batching like this. Code: simple version with global int and local ints per worker thread, atomic operations.

coding_data_structures.label: Critical Data Structure Implementations
coding_data_structures.description: Implement fundamental data structures from scratch. Understand internals, complexity, and trade-offs.

q1.label: Implement Hash Table (HashMap)
q1.description: Build a hash map from scratch with collision handling

metadata.answer: Hash Table: Map keys to values using hash function. Implementation: Array of buckets, each bucket is list (chaining). On put(key, value): hash(key) % table_size = index, find bucket at index, add/update (key,value) pair. On get(key): compute index, search bucket, return value or null. Complexity: O(1) average, O(n) worst (all keys hash to same bucket). Collision handling: (1) Chaining - list in each bucket (what we describe), (2) Open addressing - find another empty slot. Load factor = n/capacity. When load_factor > threshold (0.75), resize table (double size), rehash all entries. Code: class HashMap with put(), get(), resize(). Need: hash function (usually built-in), list of lists, size counter. Edge cases: null keys/values allowed? Handle negative hash values (mod with size). Deletes: Mark as tombstone or remove from list. Why important: Foundation for all hash structures. Interview: explain hash collision, why resize needed, time complexity analysis.
