q2.label: Implement Binary Search Tree
q2.description: Build BST with insert, search, delete operations

metadata.answer: Binary Search Tree: Each node has value, left child < value, right child > value. Operations: Insert O(log n) avg, O(n) worst (unbalanced). Search O(log n) avg. Delete O(log n) avg. Implementation: class TreeNode(val, left, right). Insert: if val < node.val go left else go right, recursively. Search: if val == node.val found, if val < node.val go left, else right. Delete: 3 cases - (1) leaf node: delete directly, (2) one child: replace with child, (3) two children: find inorder successor (min in right subtree), replace value, delete successor node. Code: recursive implementation cleaner than iterative. InOrder traversal = sorted output. Why: BST foundation for balanced trees (AVL, Red-Black). Interview: explain deletion 3 cases with examples, why O(n) worst case (linear chains), how balancing helps.

q3.label: Implement Min/Max Heap
q3.description: Build heap with bubble-up and bubble-down operations

metadata.answer: Heap: Complete binary tree where parent < children (min) or > children (max). Implementation: Array where node at index i has left child at 2i+1, right at 2i+2, parent at (i-1)/2. Operations: Push O(log n) - add to end, bubble up (compare with parent, swap if needed). Pop O(log n) - remove root, move last element to root, bubble down (compare with children, swap with smaller/larger). Build heap O(n) - call bubble down on all non-leaf nodes starting from bottom. Code: list-based, not linked. Key formulas: left=2*i+1, right=2*i+2, parent=(i-1)//2. Why: heap enables heap sort O(n log n), priority queue, median finding. Interview: walk through push/pop with example, explain why complete binary tree shape preserved, why array representation works.
metadata.python:
import heapq
def findKLargest(nums, k):
    return heapq.nlargest(k, nums)
    # Or using min-heap: heap = nums[:k]; heapq.heapify(heap)
    # for num in nums[k:]: 
    #     if num > heap[0]: heapreplace(heap, num)
    # return heap

q4.label: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
q4.description: Build trie for fast prefix searches and autocomplete

metadata.answer: Trie: Tree where each node represents character, path from root to node = prefix. Use: Autocomplete, spell check, IP routing. Implementation: class TrieNode(children dict, is_word bool). Insert word: start at root, for each char, if not in children create new node, go to child. Mark is_word=true at end. Search: traverse characters, if any missing return false. StartsWith: like search but don't check is_word. CompletePrefix: DFS from node matching prefix, collect all words. Complexity: Insert/Search/Prefix O(m) where m=word length (not dependent on number of words!). Space: O(n*m) where n=words, m=avg length. Why useful: Much faster than hash set for prefix queries. Autocomplete: given prefix, find all matching words. Code: class Trie with TrieNode, insert(), search(), startsWith(), getWordsWithPrefix(). Interview: explain space/time tradeoff vs hash set, why prefix independent of data size, walk through autocomplete implementation.

q5.label: Implement Graph (Adjacency List and Matrix)
q5.description: Build graph representation for connected data

metadata.answer: Graph: Nodes connected by edges. Representations: (1) Adjacency List - dict/list of neighbors. Space O(V+E). Good for sparse graphs. (2) Adjacency Matrix - 2D array, matrix[i][j]=weight. Space O(V²). Good for dense graphs. Adjacency List: graph = {node: [neighbors]}. Operation: add edge (u,v) O(1), get neighbors (u) O(1), check edge exists (u,v) O(neighbors of u) = O(degree). Adjacency Matrix: graph = 2D array n x n. Operation: add edge O(1), check edge O(1), iterate neighbors O(V). Weighted graphs: store (neighbor, weight) in list or weight in matrix. Directed/Undirected: directed - add edge one way, undirected - add both ways. Code: class Graph with add_edge(), get_neighbors(), has_edge(). For most problems use adjacency list unless specified otherwise. Interview: explain tradeoff sparse vs dense, how to handle weights, directed vs undirected.

coding_complexity.label: Complexity Analysis - Time and Space
coding_complexity.description: Master time and space complexity analysis. Big O notation, optimization strategies, amortized analysis.

q1.label: What is Big O notation and how to analyze?
q1.description: Understand Big O, Big Theta, Big Omega

metadata.answer: Big O: Upper bound on growth rate. f(n) = O(g(n)) means f(n) <= c*g(n) for sufficiently large n and constant c. Big Omega: Lower bound. Big Theta: Tight bound. Most interview use Big O for worst case. Common complexities (fast to slow): O(1) constant, O(log n) logarithmic, O(n) linear, O(n log n) linearithmic, O(n²) quadratic, O(n³) cubic, O(2^n) exponential, O(n!) factorial. How to analyze: (1) Count operations in loops - nested loop = multiply, (2) Drop constants - 3n is O(n), (3) Drop lower order terms - n²+n is O(n²), (4) Recursive: use recurrence relation or master theorem. Example: for i in range(n): for j in range(n): operation -> 2 nested loops = O(n²). Binary search on array of size n: O(log n) - each step halves array. Fibonacci recursive naive: O(2^n) - each call spawns 2 calls. Interview: always state complexity, justify, discuss worst vs average case.

q2.label: How to optimize from O(n²) to better?
q2.description: Techniques for improving time complexity

metadata.answer: Optimization techniques: (1) Use better data structure - array O(n) search -> hash set O(1), (2) Use different algorithm - bubble sort O(n²) -> merge sort O(n log n), (3) Precompute - if iterating same thing multiple times, compute once, (4) Memoization - cache subproblem results (DP), (5) Greedy approach - local optimal choices, (6) Mathematical insight - recognize pattern instead of brute force. Examples: Two sum O(n²) brute force -> O(n) with hash map. Longest substring without repeating O(n²) nested loops -> O(n) sliding window. Fibonacci O(2^n) naive recursive -> O(n) DP with memoization. Recognition: 'all pairs' usually means nested loops O(n²). 'Find in array' suggests hash structure O(1). 'Sorted array' suggests binary search O(log n). Ask interviewer: Can I use extra space? Time limit? This guides optimization. Interview: brainstorm multiple approaches, start brute force, then optimize, explain reasoning.

q3.label: Space complexity - when to optimize?
q3.description: Understanding and analyzing space usage

metadata.answer: Space complexity: Auxiliary space used beyond input. Common: O(1) constant, O(log n) recursion depth, O(n) array/hash, O(n²) 2D array. Rules: (1) Input space doesn't count toward space complexity, (2) Recursion depth counts - recursion O(n) uses O(n) stack space, (3) Data structures count - using hash map = O(n) space. Optimize space: (1) In-place modification if allowed (destructive, discuss with interviewer), (2) Iterative instead of recursive - saves stack space, (3) Stream processing - don't store all data, (4) Generators/iterators. Example: Reverse array in-place O(1) vs new array O(n). Merge sorted arrays in-place O(1) - complex but possible. Fibonacci iterative O(1) space vs recursive O(n) call stack. Interview: Ask 'Can I modify input?' Discuss space/time tradeoff. Usually time complexity matters more, but if space limited (mobile, embedded), optimize space. State both complexities clearly.

q4.label: Amortized complexity - when individual ops are slow
q4.description: Average cost over sequence of operations

metadata.answer: Amortized Complexity: Average cost per operation over sequence, even if individual ops slow. Example: Dynamic array append. If array full and append, resize (copy all n elements) = O(n). But happens rarely. Over n appends, resize happens at powers of 2 (1, 2, 4, 8, ...). Total work = n + n/2 + n/4 + ... = O(n) for n appends. Average per append = O(n)/n = O(1) amortized. Amortized analysis techniques: (1) Aggregate analysis - calculate total work, divide by n, (2) Accounting method - each operation has 'cost' that amortizes over sequence. Banker's algorithm for push/pop with resize. Why matters: Hash table resize, segment tree updates, other data structures. Interview: Distinguish amortized vs worst case. Append to dynamic array: O(1) amortized, O(n) worst case (when resize). When designing systems, amortized O(1) often good enough if spikes rare.
metadata.python:
import heapq
def findKLargest(nums, k):
    return heapq.nlargest(k, nums)
    # Or using min-heap: heap = nums[:k]; heapq.heapify(heap)
    # for num in nums[k:]: 
    #     if num > heap[0]: heapreplace(heap, num)
    # return heap

coding_best_practices.label: Coding Interview Best Practices
coding_best_practices.description: How to approach coding problems effectively. Problem clarification, clean code, testing, optimization.

q1.label: How to clarify a problem before coding?
q1.description: Ask the right questions to understand requirements

metadata.answer: Always clarify before coding (even if you think you understand). Questions to ask: (1) Input constraints - array size? range of numbers? (2) Edge cases - empty input? single element? duplicates? (3) Output format - return value type? modify in place? (4) Special cases - negative numbers? zeros? (5) Performance - time/space limits? (6) Examples - walk through small example to confirm understanding. Example: 'Two sum' clarifications: (1) Can there be duplicates in array? (2) Must return indices or values? (3) Any time/space constraints? (4) Guaranteed one solution or could be zero/many? (5) Can array be modified? Smart interviewer appreciates these questions - shows thoughtfulness. If problem vague, clarify or propose assumptions: 'I'm assuming array size < 10^5, is that right?' Get confirmation. Once clarified, restate: 'So the problem is: given array of integers, find two numbers that sum to target, return their indices.' Interviewer nods - you're on same page.
metadata.python:
def reverseString(s):
    left, right = 0, len(s) - 1
    while left < right:
        s[left], s[right] = s[right], s[left]
        left += 1
        right -= 1
    return s

q2.label: Always discuss approach before coding
q2.description: Plan before jumping into implementation

metadata.answer: Never jump straight into coding. Discuss approach first. Steps: (1) Explain brute force - show you understand problem, naive solution is baseline, (2) Identify bottleneck - 'brute force is O(n²), we iterate array twice. Can we optimize?' (3) Propose optimized approach - 'Use hash map for O(1) lookups, single pass = O(n)', (4) Discuss tradeoffs - 'Hash map costs O(n) space but saves time', (5) Confirm approach - 'Does this sound good?' (6) Walk through small example with your approach to verify logic. Only after all this, code. Benefits: (1) Shows you think before implementing, (2) Catches logic errors early, (3) Interviewer can guide if you're off track, (4) Easier to code once approach clear. Bad: Coding immediately, realizing approach wrong halfway through, restarting. Good: 'Let me think about this... I'll use a hash map approach because...', walk through logic, code cleanly.
metadata.python:
def maxSubArray(nums, k):
    window_sum = sum(nums[:k])
    max_sum = window_sum
    for i in range(k, len(nums)):
        window_sum += nums[i] - nums[i-k]
        max_sum = max(max_sum, window_sum)
    return max_sum

q3.label: How to write clean, readable code?
q3.description: Code quality matters in interviews

metadata.answer: Code quality signals care and professionalism. Best practices: (1) Naming - use descriptive names, 'num_pairs' not 'np', 'left_sum' not 'ls', (2) Structure - modularize, helper functions, avoid duplication, (3) Comments - add where logic non-obvious, not for every line, (4) Style - consistent indentation, spacing, follow language conventions, (5) Error handling - check for edge cases, null checks, (6) Constants - extract magic numbers, #define MAX=10^5, (7) Avoid clever tricks - readability > cleverness. Example bad: for i in range(len(a)): for j in range(i+1, len(a)): if a[i]+a[j]==t: return i,j. Example good: def find_two_sum(numbers, target): seen = set(); for i, num in enumerate(numbers): complement = target - num; if complement in seen: return seen[complement], i; seen.add(num). Readability! When coding: (1) Declare variables clearly, (2) Use meaningful variable names, (3) Add 2-3 line comment explaining key logic, (4) Format consistently. Interviewer reads your code - make it easy.

q4.label: How to test your code after writing?
q4.description: Validating correctness with test cases

metadata.answer: After coding, test before submitting: (1) Happy path - normal input, 'two sum [2,7,11,15] target 9' -> [0,1], (2) Edge cases - empty [], single element [5], two elements [1,2], (3) Special values - zeros [0,0], negatives [-1,1], duplicates [1,1,1], (4) Boundary - very large numbers, very large arrays. How to test: (1) Walk through code with example - 'num=2, complement=7, seen empty, add 2, num=7, complement=2, found! return 0,1', (2) Trace through code line by line mentally, (3) Actually run code (if online judge allows). Don't submit untested. Common bugs to catch: Off-by-one in loops, null pointer, wrong comparison operators, forgot initialization, duplicates not handled. Example: 'Two indices must be different' - check code ensures you don't return same index twice. Test also catches logic errors: Did I implement correctly what I described? Interview time limited - test efficiently. Test wrong answer quickly: 'Actually let me trace through... oh I see the bug.' Fix it. This shows attention to detail.

q5.label: When and how to optimize after getting it working?
q5.description: Refactoring for better complexity

metadata.answer: Flow: (1) Get working solution first, (2) Test it, (3) Then optimize. Don't prematurely optimize - it distracts from getting working. Interview strategy: (1) Propose initial approach, (2) Code and test, (3) Say 'It works, but it's O(n²). Let me optimize to O(n).' (4) Refactor. Optimization ideas: (1) Early termination - 'If we find answer early, return immediately', (2) Different data structure - 'Hash instead of nested loop', (3) Reduce iterations - 'Two pointers instead of two loops', (4) Memoize - 'Store computed results', (5) Mathematical insight - 'Formula instead of loop'. When stuck optimizing: (1) Discuss with interviewer 'I have O(n²) solution. What if I use a hash map for O(1) lookups?' (2) If interviewer hints at optimization, take it and run with it, (3) If can't optimize in time, submit better solution - even if not optimal, showing approach matters. Common: O(n²) solution is acceptable if n < 10^4. If n=10^6, need better. Ask constraints! Optimization should be justifiable - 'This saves time complexity from O(n²) to O(n log n)' with brief explanation.

coding_strategies.label: Problem-Solving Strategies When Stuck
coding_strategies.description: Techniques for handling difficult problems and time management in interviews.

q1.label: What to do when completely stuck on problem?
q1.description: Debugging strategy when no approach comes to mind

metadata.answer: If stuck: (1) Don't panic - silence is bad, think out loud, (2) Restate problem - 'So I need to find... Got it.' (3) Start with brute force - even if inefficient, having something is better than nothing, 'I could check all pairs...' (4) Think about constraints - 'This is about arrays, maybe two pointers?' (5) Look for patterns - 'Similar to problem I've seen before', (6) Ask interviewer - 'Can I ask a hint?' They give hint, follow it, (7) Reduce problem - 'What if array size 2? 3?' Work through manually, see if pattern emerges, (8) Walk through example - manually work through example with brute force, maybe optimization appears, (9) Consider data structures - 'What data structure helps?' Hash? Heap? Tree? (10) If truly stuck, code brute force, test it, then brainstorm optimizations. Interview values effort and communication. Better: 'Stuck. Let me think of brute force... Ah, I can check all pairs, that's O(n²)...' Than: 30 seconds silent, then 'I don't know.' Don't be afraid to think out loud. Interviewer might drop hint or see you're on right track.

q2.label: Communication - thinking out loud
q2.description: How to explain your thought process during interviews

metadata.answer: Communication is critical - interviewer can't read your mind. Think out loud: (1) 'This problem wants two numbers summing to target. Brute force is check all pairs, that's O(n²).' (2) 'Wait, if I store seen numbers in a hash, I can do single pass... when I see current number, check if complement exists.' (3) 'So approach: iterate array, for each number, check if (target - number) in hash, add current to hash.' (4) 'This is O(n) time, O(n) space.' (5) Walk through example. Benefits: (1) Interviewer understands you, (2) If stuck, they can guide, (3) If on wrong track, they correct early, (4) Shows methodical thinking. Don't: Sit silent coding, then show code hoping it works. Do: Talk while coding - 'Now I'm initializing hash map... iterating through array... checking if complement exists...' This way interviewer follows. Also, asking questions signals engagement: 'Does this approach sound right?' 'Should I add error handling here?' 'Can array have duplicates?' Interviewer appreciates it. Communication can save you - even if code has bugs, good explanation shows understanding. 'The logic is right, I just wrote x instead of y by accident' = much better than silently submitting buggy code.

q3.label: Time management in coding interviews
q3.description: Allocating time effectively across problem

metadata.answer: Typical 45-minute interview: (1) Clarification & approach: 10-15 min. Ask questions, propose approach, get confirmation. (2) Coding: 20-25 min. Write working solution. Don't rush, readable code matters. (3) Testing & optimization: 5-10 min. Test with examples, discuss optimization. Timing varies - complex problems need more clarification. Simple problems need more optimization time. General rule: Spend time upfront on planning. Saves time coding. If rushing code, introduce bugs, waste time debugging. Better: 15 min planning + 20 min clean coding = working solution. Than: 3 min planning + 30 min coding = buggy code, debugging, incomplete. If running out of time: (1) Finish what you're coding, make sure it compiles/runs, (2) Explain what optimization you'd do if more time, (3) Better to have working suboptimal than incomplete optimal. Don't: (1) Spend 30 min on one edge case, (2) Over-optimize at cost of finishing, (3) Code without thinking. Time management = discipline. Watch your clock. At 20 min mark, should have approach settled. At 35 min, should have working code. Last 10 min, test and optimize.

company_products.label: Company Products & Services Overview
company_products.description: Understand what the company does, how they make money, their users, competition, and market position.

q1.label: What is the company's core business and main revenue streams?
q1.description: Identify primary products and how they monetize

metadata.answer: Core business: What problem does the company solve? Who pays for it? Example Netflix: Core = streaming video content. Revenue = monthly subscriptions ($15/month), ad tier. Not: DVD rentals (legacy). How to research: (1) Company website homepage - says mission/products, (2) Financial filings (10-K for public companies) - revenue breakdown, (3) Wikipedia 'Business' section, (4) Recent press releases. What to understand: (1) Primary revenue vs side projects, (2) Customers - consumers or B2B?, (3) Subscription vs one-time vs ads, (4) Growth areas (where is investment?). Example Google: Core = search advertising (90% revenue). Secondary = cloud, YouTube, hardware. Why matters: Interview questions about growth/challenges reference primary business. Showing you understand their business model = respect for company. For System Design rounds: 'Netflix handles X subscribers' questions common. Behavioral: 'Why Netflix?' answer better if you mention their content strategy, technology scale, etc.

q1.label: What languages and frameworks does the company use?

metadata.answer: Tech stack: What programming languages? Frameworks? Example Netflix: Backend = Java, Node.js; Frontend = React; Infrastructure = AWS. How to research: (1) Company engineering blog - technical posts reveal stack, (2) Job postings - required skills hint at stack, (3) GitHub public repos (if they open source), (4) YouTube engineering talks. What to look for: (1) Primary language vs secondary, (2) Monolith vs microservices, (3) Frontend framework (React? Vue? Angular?), (4) Database (SQL? NoSQL?), (5) Cloud provider (AWS? GCP? Azure?). Example Twitter: Scala (backend), TypeScript (frontend), AWS, PostgreSQL. Why matters: (1) Job interviews might ask about their specific tech, 'Have you used Java?' (2) System design answers should reference their tech - 'I'd use their Kafka infrastructure for...' (3) Shows respect for their engineering choices. Ask: 'What's the primary language for backend?' Interviewer impressed if you know. Research: Look at company engineering blog for articles - 'How we built X with [language]' is common. These reveal real stack and challenges.

company_scale.label: Scale & Technical Challenges

q1.label: What are the company's scale metrics (users, QPS, data volume)?
q1.description: Quantifying the scale of their systems

metadata.answer: Scale metrics: Daily active users? Monthly active users? Queries per second (QPS)? Data volume? Example Twitter: 500M DAU, 20K tweets/sec, 1.5 petabytes stored. How to research: (1) Investor presentations (quarterly earnings) - often mention user metrics, (2) Company blog - 'Handling X at scale' articles give hints, (3) Wikipedia 'Scale' sections, (4) Conference talks - engineers mention metrics casually. What metrics matter: (1) DAU/MAU (daily/monthly active users) - user base size, (2) QPS (queries per second) - system throughput, (3) Data volume - storage needs, (4) Geographic distribution - how many countries? (5) Peak vs average - peak load is more challenging. Example: LinkedIn has 900M members, but DAU maybe 300M. Tweets per day 500M+. Google handles 8.5B searches/day. Why matters: (1) System design questions based on real scale - 'Design like Twitter to handle 20K tweets/sec', (2) Understanding scale explains architecture choices - 'Netflix uses Cassandra because single DB can't handle their write volume', (3) Shows you appreciate engineering challenges. Interview signal: 'I know you handle 500M daily users, so I'd design with horizontal scaling in mind.'

q3.label: How has their architecture evolved to handle growth?

metadata.answer: Architecture evolution: How did they start? How did they scale? Example Instagram: Started simple (Django/PostgreSQL). As users grew (1M -> 100M -> 2B), moved to distributed systems, microservices, DynamoDB, cache layers (Memcached). Timeline matters: (1) Early stage - simple architecture (monolith), (2) Growing - distributed but still DB-heavy, (3) Scale - microservices, read replicas, caching, (4) Hyper-scale - data centers, custom solutions. How to research: (1) Company blog has 'lessons learned' posts about scaling, (2) YouTube talks: '[Company] architecture at scale', (3) Conference presentations (re:Invent for AWS, GCP Summit, etc.). What to understand: (1) Why changes? Netflix moved from monolith to microservices because monolith couldn't scale independently, (2) What problems did each change solve? Moving to distributed DB solved write scale but introduced consistency challenges, (3) Current state - what's their architecture now? Example Instagram's current: Microservices, DynamoDB, Cassandra, Redis, Kafka. How they evolved to that: (1) Monolith -> microservices (independence), (2) Single DB -> distributed (scale), (3) No cache -> cache layers (speed). Why matters: (1) Interview design questions often reference their current architecture, (2) Behavioral: 'I'm excited to work on a company scaling from X to Y users', (3) Signals understanding of tradeoffs - you know why they made choices. Interview signal: 'Your evolution from monolith to microservices mirrors industry best practices for scaling.'

Why matters: (1) Shows you're interested and informed, (2) Predicts interview themes - if they bought a payment company, expect payments questions, (3) Helps answer 'Why us?' - 'Your recent acquisition into X market aligns with my background', (4) Conversation starters - 'I saw you announced Y, how is that impacting your team?' company_culture.label: Culture, Values & Work Style
company_culture.description: Understand company mission, values, work environment, and employee satisfaction.

q1.label: What are the company's stated mission and core values?
q1.description: Understanding what company stands for

metadata.answer: Mission & values: What is company's purpose? What do they believe? How to research: (1) Company website 'About' or 'Mission' page, (2) Leadership letters to employees/investors, (3) Diversity/inclusion statements, (4) Company handbook (if public). What to understand: (1) Mission - company's why (example Patagonia: 'Protect the environment'), (2) Values - how they operate (example Google: 'Don't be evil' - though controversial), (3) How lived vs stated - do employees agree? Check Glassdoor. Why matters: (1) Behavioral interviews test culture fit - 'Tell me about a time you acted on your values', (2) Show alignment - if company values environmental sustainability and you do, mention it, (3) Red flag if stated values conflict with reality - check Glassdoor. Example: Company claims 'family-friendly' but Glassdoor says 'work 80-hour weeks'. Interview signal: 'Your mission to democratize X aligns with my goal to...' Shows thoughtfulness. Red flag: You can't articulate company's mission. Do basic research.

q2.label: What is the work-life balance and remote work policy?
q2.description: Understanding day-to-day work environment

metadata.answer: Work environment: Remote? Office? Hybrid? Work-life balance? How to research: (1) Glassdoor reviews - employees rate 'work-life balance' (1-5), (2) Blind discussions - current employees share anonymously, (3) Job postings mention 'remote' or 'office required', (4) Company culture page (Airbnb, Notion show this), (5) LinkedIn alumni - look at current employees' updates. What to understand: (1) Remote % (fully remote, hybrid, in-office), (2) Expectations - always on-call? Flexible hours? (3) Crunch seasons - annual cycles of intensity? (4) Vacation time - stated vs taken, (5) Flexible work - can you adjust hours? Work 4-day weeks? What to listen for: (1) 'We trust you to work when you're most productive' = flexible, (2) 'Early birds get the worm' = sync meetings early, (3) 'We work hard and play hard' = intense work + social culture, (4) 'Balance is important' = could mean anything, check Glassdoor for proof. Red flags: (1) Glassdoor reviews say 'burnout', (2) Employees mention 'always on-call', (3) No parental leave, (4) Time off frowned upon. Green flags: (1) Glassdoor 4+ for 'work-life balance', (2) Employees mention taking vacation, (3) Generous leave (20+ days), (4) Remote/flexible options. Interview signal: 'I saw you offer remote flexibility, which aligns with my work style.'

q3.label: How does the company approach diversity, inclusion, and growth?
q3.description: DEI efforts and career development

metadata.answer: Diversity & Inclusion: How diverse is company? Any DEI initiatives? Mentorship? Promotion from within? How to research: (1) LinkedIn - filter company employees by demographics, rough diversity sense, (2) Company diversity reports (many publish), (3) Glassdoor - employees rate 'diversity and inclusion' (1-5), (4) Blind - anonymous discussions about DEI efforts, (5) News articles - company's DEI commitments. Career growth: Does company promote from within? Mentorship programs? Training budget? How to research: (1) Glassdoor - 'Career development' rating, (2) LinkedIn - see career paths of current employees, (3) Ask current employees 'How did you advance?', (4) Job postings - do they hire externally for every role or promote internally? What to understand: (1) Diversity numbers (rough gender/ethnic/age diversity), (2) Are diverse employees in leadership or just hiring level?, (3) Mentorship culture - do senior people invest in junior?, (4) Promotions - how long to advance?, (5) Learning budget - $ for courses/conferences. Red flags: (1) Glassdoor low DEI scores, (2) All leadership looks same demographic, (3) No diversity report (might be hiding something), (4) 'Culture fit' emphasis (can mean same-ness), (5) Slow promotions (5+ years to next level). Green flags: (1) Diverse leadership, (2) Public diversity report, (3) Mentorship programs, (4) Internal mobility (people move departments/advance), (5) Learning budgets. Interview signal: 'I'm committed to working on teams with diverse perspectives, I see that here.'

company_interview_patterns.label: Interview Patterns & Question Types
company_interview_patterns.description: Understand what to expect in company interviews and what they focus on.

q1.label: What is the typical interview process (rounds, types)?
q1.description: Understanding interview structure

metadata.answer: Interview process: How many rounds? What types (phone screen, coding, system design, behavioral)? How to research: (1) Glassdoor - 'Interview process' section shows typical flow, (2) Blind discussions - current/past candidates share experience, (3) LeetCode company forum, (4) Ask referrals 'What was your interview?', (5) Company careers page sometimes describes process. Typical patterns: (1) Phone screen (30 min) - culture/basics, (2) Coding round (60 min) - algorithm/data structure, (3) System design (60 min) - architecture for their scale, (4) Behavioral (30-60 min) - team fit, (5) Team match/manager round (30 min) - specific role discussion. Some variations: (1) Google - coding + system design rounds separate, (2) Netflix - system design heavy, (3) Microsoft - practical coding (less algorithm, more building), (4) Facebook - 2+ coding rounds, 1+ system design. What to prepare: (1) Research tells you what to focus - if they have 3 coding rounds, practice harder, (2) Coding-heavy vs system design-heavy matters, (3) Number of rounds = time commitment. Why matters: (1) Tailor prep to their pattern, (2) Know what to expect reduces anxiety, (3) Can ask 'What should I focus on?' Interview signal: 'I understand you have a system design round, I've been preparing for distributed systems challenges.'

q2.label: What company-specific or domain-specific questions appear?
q2.description: Company-themed interview questions

metadata.answer: Company-specific questions: Do they ask about their specific products/problems? How to research: (1) Glassdoor - often includes example questions, (2) Blind - search '[Company] interview' for candidates sharing questions, (3) LeetCode forum under company tag, (4) YouTube - search '[Company] interview experience', (5) Referral feedback - ask what they were asked. Domain-specific focus: (1) Netflix heavy on distributed systems (streaming at scale), (2) Google heavy on algorithms (search, ranking), (3) Facebook/Meta heavy on large-scale data (recommendations, feed), (4) Stripe heavy on payments/transactions, (5) Uber heavy on real-time systems (matching). Pattern: Design questions often reference actual company challenges. Example: 'Design recommendation system like Netflix' for Netflix, 'Design Stripe's payment processing' for Stripe. What to prepare: (1) Understand company's main challenge, (2) Practice system design on that domain, (3) Know their scale/constraints (Netflix = 250M users, want <2sec load time), (4) Read their blog for technical insights. Interview signal: In system design round, you can reference their actual architecture - 'I know Netflix uses Cassandra, so I'd design similarly...'

q3.label: What do they emphasize - coding vs system design vs behavioral?
q3.description: Understanding company's interview priorities

metadata.answer: Interview focus: Which rounds matter most? How much emphasis on each type? How to research: (1) Glassdoor candidate feedback - 'Most intense was system design round', (2) Job description - lists required skills (coding-heavy? design-heavy?), (3) Recent hire feedback - what mattered most?, (4) Company blog mentioning interview. Emphasis patterns: (1) Early-stage startup: Coding + culture fit (can they code?), (2) Scale-up: Coding + system design equally, (3) FAANG: All three equally strong, (4) Infrastructure company: System design heavy, (5) Startup on growth: Practical coding (ship features). Why focuses vary: (1) Netflix system design heavy because core challenge is scale, (2) Google algorithm heavy because search is algorithmic, (3) Startups practical coding because need to ship fast. How to adapt prep: (1) System design heavy company? Deep dive into their architecture, (2) Coding heavy? Practice more algorithm problems, (3) Behavioral heavy? Practice stories, company research. What Glassdoor tells you: If reviews say 'Easy coding, hard system design' = focus there. If 'Multiple coding rounds' = practice more. Interview signal: 'Based on your interview feedback, I see system design is key, so I've focused there.'  And you should have.

company_checklist.label: Company Research Checklist & Timeline
company_checklist.description: Practical checklist and timeline for researching a company before interviews.

q1.label: What is the 2-hour research checklist per company?
q1.description: Quick research plan for any company

metadata.answer: 2-hour company research checklist (spread over 1 week): (1) Mission & Products (15 min): Read company homepage, understand what they do, main revenue streams. (2) Recent news (10 min): Google '[Company] news', scan last month of announcements. (3) Tech stack (15 min): Search '[Company] engineering blog' for 'tech stack' posts, find languages/databases/infrastructure. (4) Scale metrics (10 min): Find user count, QPS, data volume (look at investor presentations, Wikipedia). (5) Interview feedback (20 min): Glassdoor 'Interview' section, read 5-10 recent reviews, note common question types. (6) Culture & work environment (15 min): Glassdoor ratings, Blind discussions, look for work-life balance feedback. (7) Technical challenges (10 min): Read 2-3 company blog posts about challenges they're solving. (8) Competitive landscape (10 min): Who competes with them? Why are they different? (9) Prepare 3-5 questions (15 min): Write questions you'd ask interviewer about role/team/technical challenges. Total: ~2 hours. When to do: (1) 1 week before interviews (if remote), (2) 2 weeks if traveling, (3) 3 weeks if multiple companies. Why 2 hours: (1) Enough to be informed without overwhelming, (2) Shows effort without obsession, (3) Enough to answer 'Why us?' genuinely. Why timeline matters: (1) Recent research stays fresh, (2) Too early = forget details, (3) Night before = no time to digest.

q2.label: When to do company research relative to interviews?
q2.description: Timeline and pacing for research

metadata.answer: Research timeline: When to research relative to interview dates? (1) Phone screen (initial screen): Research day before, basics only - know mission, products, main challenge. Don't need deep dive yet. Focus: Why company? Why role? (2) Coding rounds: Can research while preparing general coding. Baseline knowledge enough. Focus: Company's scale for system design thinking. (3) System design round: Deep research 3 days before. Need to reference their architecture. Focus: Their tech stack, known technical challenges, scale metrics. (4) Final round (usually with hiring manager): Fresh research day before. Focus: Specific team, their challenges, how you fit. Practical schedule: (1) Day you get interview scheduled: Quick research (30 min) - understand basic business. (2) 1 week before: Deep research (2 hours) - tech stack, challenges, interview patterns. (3) 3 days before system design round: Re-read their architecture, practice their scale. (4) Day before final: Research specific team/role. Why this spread: (1) Info stays fresh when closer to interview, (2) Allows time to digest, (3) Reduces cramming (bad for retention), (4) Lets you practice with their constraints in mind. Exception: Multiple companies? Research when you get schedule notification, then organize by interview date. Interview signal: Pacing matters - someone who researched week ago might forget details. Recent research = better stories, better examples.

q3.label: How to take notes and remember what you learned?
q3.description: Organizing research for retention and reference

metadata.answer: Documentation strategy: How to track research so you remember? (1) Simple approach: Google Doc with sections. Write down: Mission, Scale (users/QPS), Tech (languages/databases), Challenges, News, Interview patterns, Questions for them. (2) Better approach: Template per company with same sections. Makes pattern clear. (3) Review before each round: Read your notes 30 min before interview. (4) Update as you learn: After recruiter call, add new info. After first interview, note what they emphasized. Reference system: (1) Highlight key stats - '500M users' should pop out visually, (2) Bullet points not paragraphs - easier to scan, (3) Links to sources - if they ask where you learned something, you know. Example notes structure: NETFLIX Research ---- Mission: Make people happy by streaming entertainment Users: 250M DAU, 1.5 petabytes stored Tech stack: Java, React, Cassandra, AWS, Redis Challenges: (1) Real-time streaming, (2) Personalization at scale, (3) Resilience Interview type: 2 coding + 1 system design + 1 behavioral Questions for them: (1) How do you A/B test recommendations? (2) What's your approach to preventing outages? Note-taking benefits: (1) Forced to organize thoughts (helps understanding), (2) Reference during interviews - you can glance at notes beforehand, (3) Comparison across companies - which one excites you most?, (4) Interview signal if they ask source - 'I read about this in your Q3 earnings call.'

company_smart_questions.label: Smart Questions to Ask Interviewers
company_smart_questions.description: Thoughtful questions that show genuine interest and strategic thinking.

q1.label: What technical questions show deep interest and understanding?
q1.description: Questions about their architecture and technical challenges

metadata.answer: Technical questions (great for engineering interviews): (1) 'What's your biggest technical challenge right now?' (open-ended, signals interest), (2) 'How are you scaling [specific metric]?' (shows you know their scale), (3) 'I saw you use Cassandra, what drove that choice over X?' (shows research + curiosity about tradeoffs), (4) 'How do you balance consistency vs availability?' (systems thinking), (5) 'What's your approach to preventing the kinds of outages you've had in the past?' (strategic thinking), (6) 'How do you handle the complexity of your microservices?' (knows they use microservices), (7) 'What's your incident response process?' (cares about reliability). Good patterns: (1) Reference something specific you learned (shows research), (2) Ask about tradeoffs (shows depth), (3) Ask about their philosophy (shows interest in thinking, not just tools). Bad questions: (1) 'What languages do you use?' (you should know from research), (2) 'Tell me about your tech stack' (homework question), (3) Generic 'What tools do you use?' Question strategy: Ask 1-2 technical questions (especially in system design or technical rounds), saves others for behavioral. Best answered by: Engineer on the team (not HR), so time it for technical interviews.

q2.label: What team and culture questions reveal fit?
q2.description: Questions about working environment and team dynamics

metadata.answer: Team & Culture questions: (1) 'What does your team dynamic look like?' (open, non-confrontational), (2) 'How do you approach learning and growth on the team?' (you care about development), (3) 'How do you handle disagreements on technical decisions?' (cares about collaboration), (4) 'What's it like to be new on this team?' (practical, relevant), (5) 'What made you join this company?' (asks for their honest perspective), (6) 'What's your favorite thing about working here?' (looks for authentic answer, not script), (7) 'How does your team balance shipping fast vs technical debt?' (strategic thinking), (8) 'What does a typical week look like?' (understands workflow), (9) 'How is success measured in this role?' (cares about clear expectations). Good patterns: (1) Open questions (not yes/no), (2) Ask for their personal experience, not company line, (3) Signals you'll be collaborative, (4) Shows you think about culture fit both ways. Bad questions: (1) 'Do you have work-life balance?' (sounds accusatory), (2) 'What's your company culture?' (generic), (3) 'Is the work hard?' Question strategy: Ask 2-3 of these across interviews (behavioral round best), especially in final round with manager. Best answered by: Hiring manager or team members (not HR, they give script answers).

Bad questions: (1) 'Why did you acquire X?' (sounds critical if you disapprove), (2) 'Aren't you bleeding money?' (accusatory) Question strategy: Ask 1 strategic question in final round to leave strong impression. Best answered by: Hiring manager or senior engineer (they think strategically). Interview signal: Someone who researches and asks thoughtful questions stands out. Most candidates ask generic questions. You won't.

infra_devops.label: Infrastructure & DevOps
infra_devops.description: Kubernetes, Docker, CI/CD pipelines, infrastructure as code, monitoring and deployment at scale.

metadata.tags: kubernetes, docker, cicd, devops, infrastructure, deployment, terraform

q1.label: Kubernetes Architecture & Pod Lifecycle
q1.description: Explain Kubernetes architecture and how pods are scheduled and managed across nodes.

metadata.answer: Kubernetes uses master nodes with API server, scheduler, and controller manager. Worker nodes run kubelet and kube-proxy. When you deploy a pod, the scheduler examines resource requests, node affinity rules, and taints/tolerations to place it optimally. The kubelet on each node pulls the container image and starts it. For high availability, we use multiple master nodes with etcd for state. At Netflix, we manage thousands of pods across multiple clusters with custom admission controllers for resource enforcement.

q2.label: Design a CI/CD Pipeline for Microservices
q2.description: Design a complete CI/CD architecture that handles builds, tests, security scanning, and deployment for microservices.

metadata.answer: Pipeline stages: (1) Code commit triggers webhook to CI system (Jenkins/GitLab CI). (2) Build stage: lint, unit tests, build Docker image. (3) Security: SAST scan (SonarQube), container image scan (Trivy), dependency check. (4) Registry push: tag image with commit SHA, push to private registry. (5) Deploy to staging: run integration tests, smoke tests. (6) Manual approval gate. (7) Canary deploy to prod: 10% traffic for 1hr, monitor metrics, then full rollout. At Uber, we do this 1000+ times/day with automated rollbacks on SLO breach.

q3.label: Infrastructure as Code & Configuration Management
q3.description: How would you manage infrastructure at scale using IaC? What tools and patterns do you use?

metadata.answer: Use Terraform for infrastructure definition, versioned in Git. Separate environments: dev, staging, prod. Each environment has its own state file (remote backend in S3). Use modules for reusable components (VPC, Kubernetes cluster, RDS). For application config, use ConfigMaps and Secrets in Kubernetes, or Consul/etcd for central config. At Amazon, we use CloudFormation as IaC, with drift detection to ensure actual state matches declared. Every change goes through code review and runs terraform plan before apply.

q4.label: Monitoring, Logging, and Observability
q4.description: How do you design monitoring and observability for large distributed systems?

metadata.answer: Three pillars: metrics, logs, traces. Metrics: Prometheus scrapes endpoints every 15s, stores time-series. Use Grafana for dashboards. Set up alerts on SLIs (latency p99, error rate, availability). Logs: structured JSON logs to ELK/Datadog, indexed for quick search. Traces: distributed tracing with Jaeger/Zipkin shows request path across services. At Google, we track every request end-to-end with trace IDs, correlate with logs and metrics. This enables rapid MTTR when something breaks.

q5.label: Container Security & Image Management
q5.description: What security practices do you implement for containers and container images?

metadata.answer: Build minimal base images (Alpine, distroless). Scan images for CVEs before pushing to registry. Use image signing (Notary, Cosign). In Kubernetes, enforce NetworkPolicies to restrict pod-to-pod traffic, use PodSecurityPolicies to block privileged containers. Secrets never in images: use Kubernetes Secrets or external vaults. Run containers as non-root. At Microsoft, we use Azure Container Registry with content trust enabled, vulnerability scanning on push, and automatic image quarantine on high-severity CVEs.

q6.label: Scaling Strategies: Horizontal & Vertical
q6.description: How do you scale Kubernetes deployments? Explain HPA, VPA, and cluster autoscaling.

metadata.answer: Horizontal Pod Autoscaling (HPA): set CPU target (e.g., 70%), Metrics Server watches pods, scales replicas up/down. Vertical Pod Autoscaling: recommends resource requests based on usage history. Cluster autoscaling: when pods can't fit on nodes, cluster autoscaler adds nodes. At Lyft, we combine all three: HPA for app replicas, cluster autoscaler for nodes, with predictive scaling based on traffic patterns from previous weeks. We also use pod disruption budgets to ensure graceful drain during scale-down.

databases.label: Databases & Data Storage
databases.description: SQL/NoSQL design, replication, partitioning, indexing, consistency models and distributed storage.

metadata.tags: databases, sql, nosql, replication, partitioning, indexing, consistency, cap-theorem

q1.label: SQL vs NoSQL: When to Choose Each
q1.description: What are the trade-offs between SQL and NoSQL databases? How do you decide which to use?

metadata.answer: SQL databases: strong ACID guarantees, normalized schemas, good for financial/transactional data. Join queries across tables. Examples: PostgreSQL, MySQL. Downsides: horizontal scaling hard (sharding complex), schema changes risky on large tables. NoSQL databases: eventual consistency, denormalized documents, horizontal scaling easy. Examples: MongoDB (document), DynamoDB (key-value), Cassandra (wide-column). At Facebook, we use MySQL for social graph (strong consistency needed) but HBase for user activity feeds (eventually consistent, massive scale). Choice depends on data shape and consistency requirements.

q2.label: Database Replication & Consistency Models
q2.description: Explain replication strategies and the trade-offs between consistency and availability.

metadata.answer: Master-slave replication: writes to master, slaves replicate asynchronously. Reads can go to slaves for scale. Downside: replication lag, slaves may serve stale data. Master-master: both nodes accept writes, reconcile asynchronously. Higher availability but conflict resolution complexity. CAP theorem: pick 2 of Consistency, Availability, Partition-tolerance. For user-facing reads, we accept eventual consistency to maximize availability. At Twitter, timeline data replicates with 200ms lag across regions - acceptable for feeds but unacceptable for critical operations like fraud detection.

q3.label: Partitioning & Sharding at Scale
q3.description: How do you partition data across multiple nodes? What are the pitfalls?

metadata.answer: Sharding: split data by key range, user ID hash, or geographic location. Each shard holds subset of data. Queries hitting single shard are efficient. Pitfalls: uneven distribution (hot shards), resharding when adding capacity, cross-shard joins become expensive. At Instagram, early version sharded by user_id: insert photo for user 1 goes to shard 1. When one shard fills up, we had to reshard entire dataset offline. Learned to pre-shard more aggressively. Modern approach: use consistent hashing so adding shard only redistributes ~1/n of data.

q4.label: Indexing Strategy for Query Performance
q4.description: How do you design database indexes for performance? What about trade-offs?

metadata.answer: B-tree indexes for range queries (MySQL default). Hash indexes for exact match. Composite indexes for multi-column queries. Every index adds write latency and disk space. At LinkedIn, for member profile queries (first_name, last_name, company), we created composite index on (company, last_name, first_name) in that order. Query planner can use index prefix. Covered indexes store selected columns in index itself, avoiding table lookup. We analyze slow query logs, add indexes for top slow queries, then monitor write latency impact.

q5.label: Distributed Transactions & Consistency
q5.description: How do you maintain consistency across multiple databases in distributed systems?

metadata.answer: Two-phase commit (2PC): coordinator asks all participants to prepare, then commits all or aborts all. Problem: blocks during commit phase, not partition-tolerant. Saga pattern: sequence of local transactions with compensating transactions for rollback. Example: payment saga - charge card (txn1), create order (txn2), allocate inventory (txn3). If (3) fails, compensate by cancelling order and refunding card. At Amazon, we use sagas extensively for order processing. Eventual consistency: accept that systems eventually converge, use event sourcing to rebuild state.

q6.label: Time-Series Databases & Analytics
q6.description: How do you design databases for time-series data at massive scale?

metadata.answer: Time-series databases (InfluxDB, Prometheus, TimescaleDB) optimize for append-only writes and time-range queries. Store metrics: (timestamp, labels, value). Data typically rolls off after 90 days. Compression: consecutive similar values compress to few bytes. At AWS, CloudWatch ingests 1M metrics/second using time-series design. Downsampling: keep raw data 1 day, hourly aggregates 30 days, daily aggregates 1 year. Pre-aggregation reduces query time. LSM trees (used in RocksDB) more efficient than B-trees for write-heavy workloads because bulk of writes hit in-memory, flush to disk in batches.

realtime.label: Real-Time Systems
realtime.description: Kafka, RabbitMQ, stream processing, pub/sub patterns, event sourcing and real-time data pipelines.

metadata.tags: kafka, rabbitmq, stream-processing, pubsub, event-sourcing, real-time, data-pipelines

q1.label: Kafka Architecture & Consumer Groups
q1.description: Explain Kafka's architecture and how consumer groups provide scalability.

metadata.answer: Kafka: distributed log platform. Topics split into partitions, each partition has replicas. Producers write to topics, consumers read. Consumer group: multiple consumers reading from same topic, each partition assigned to one consumer. If we have 3 consumers in group and 3 partitions, each consumer gets 1 partition - parallel processing. If consumer crashes, partition reassigns to another consumer. At LinkedIn (who created Kafka), we use consumer groups for data pipelines: one group processes events for analytics, another for real-time recommendations. Offsets track consumption progress, stored in Kafka or Zookeeper.

q2.label: Stream Processing: Windowing & State Management
q2.description: How do you process continuous data streams with windowing and stateful operations?

metadata.answer: Windowing: tumbling windows (non-overlapping, e.g., 1-min windows), sliding windows (overlapping, e.g., 10-min window every 1min), session windows (closed after inactivity). Example: detect when user_id has 10+ logins in 1-hour window. State management: store intermediate results - user_id -> login_count. With distributed processing, state needs checkpoint (write to persistent store periodically). Watermarks: handle late data - allow data up to 2 minutes late before closing window. At Uber, we use Flink for real-time alerts: window on (driver_id, 10-min), compute avg_speed, alert if < 5mph for > 5min (indicates crash).

q3.label: Exactly-Once Processing Semantics
q3.description: What does exactly-once processing mean and how do you achieve it?

metadata.answer: Semantics: at-most-once (data may be lost), at-least-once (may process twice), exactly-once (processed exactly once). Exactly-once requires: (1) idempotent writes - if process same message twice, result is same. Example: increment counter by 1 is NOT idempotent (gives wrong result), but SET counter=5 is idempotent. (2) Distributed transactions or sagas. (3) Offset management: store offset AFTER processing, not before. At Amazon, order processing uses exactly-once: dedup by order_id, if duplicate received, return cached response instead of re-processing. Kafka transactions support exactly-once within Kafka ecosystem: all messages processed or none.

q4.label: Event Sourcing & CQRS Pattern
q4.description: What is event sourcing and how does CQRS complement it?

metadata.answer: Event sourcing: store immutable events instead of current state. Example: for user profile, store [created_account, updated_email, added_phone, deleted_phone] instead of just final state. Replay events to reconstruct state at any point. Benefits: audit trail, time-travel debugging, eventual consistency. CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation): separate write model (command) from read model (query). Write: apply commands to event store. Read: maintain materialized views (cache) from event log. Example: order events to Kafka, separate consumer builds "order_summary" cache for fast reads. At Event Store (founders of event sourcing), temporal queries on event log answer questions like "how many active users 3 days ago?" in milliseconds.

q5.label: Pub/Sub Patterns & Decoupling
q5.description: How do you use pub/sub to decouple services?

metadata.answer: Pub/sub decouples producer and consumer: producer doesn't know who consumes. Example: user signup event published to topic, multiple services subscribe: email service sends welcome email, analytics service logs signup, fraud service checks for suspicious pattern. Publishers never blocked waiting for consumers. RabbitMQ (traditional message queue) vs Kafka (event log): RabbitMQ deletes messages after consumption, good for transient commands. Kafka keeps messages for retention period, good for event log. At Twitter, millions of tweets published to topic, consumed by many services: search indexing, recommendation, analytics. Pub/sub enables adding new consumers without changing publisher.

q6.label: Handling Backpressure & Circuit Breakers
q6.description: How do you handle slow consumers and prevent system overload?

metadata.answer: Backpressure: when consumer can't keep up, slow down producer. Kafka: consumer lag (offset behind latest) signals overload. Add more consumers to partition (horizontal scale). If all consumers overloaded, producer rate-limits or buffers. Circuit breaker: when downstream service slow/failing, stop sending requests. Example: payment service taking 30sec/request, circuit breaker immediately returns error instead of blocking. Prevents cascading failures. At Netflix, Hystrix circuit breaker: if error rate > 50% in 10sec window, open circuit (fail fast), half-open after 30sec to test recovery. Combined: slow stream processing + circuit breaker on downstream calls = resilient system.

distributed.label: Distributed Systems
distributed.description: Consensus algorithms, Byzantine faults, clock synchronization, network partitions and consistency models.

metadata.tags: distributed-systems, consensus, raft, paxos, byzantine, clock-sync, partitions

q1.label: Consensus Algorithms: Raft vs Paxos
q1.description: Explain consensus algorithms and their role in distributed systems.

metadata.answer: Consensus: multiple nodes agree on value despite failures. Raft (easier to understand): leader elected, leader proposes values, followers replicate. If majority replicate, value committed. Leader failure triggers new election. Paxos (theoretically proven): learner proposes, acceptors accept, proposers coordinate. More complex protocol. Both tolerate up to (n-1)/2 failures (need majority). Raft used in etcd (Kubernetes state store), Consul (service discovery). Paxos used in Google Chubby. At Stripe, we use Raft for financial transaction logs: if leader fails, minority follow, new leader elected, transactions continue without data loss.

q2.label: Byzantine Fault Tolerance & Quorum-Based Systems
q2.description: What is Byzantine fault tolerance and when is it necessary?

metadata.answer: Byzantine fault: node behaves arbitrarily (crashes, lies, corrupts). Byzantine fault tolerance (BFT) requires 2f+1 nodes to tolerate f faulty nodes. Example: 5 nodes tolerate 2 faults (doctors voting on diagnosis - 2 may vote maliciously, 3 voting correctly = majority). Quorum-based: wait for majority (n/2 + 1) before committing. If network splits, partition with majority continues. Partition without majority halts (prevents split-brain). At Satoshi Nakamoto (Bitcoin), Byzantine fault tolerance enables 51% consensus without identity: anyone can be faulty, but if 51% honest, system reaches consensus. Most enterprise systems assume fail-stop (don't lie), use regular consensus; blockchain uses BFT because nodes untrusted.

q3.label: Clock Synchronization & Logical Clocks
q3.description: How do you order events across machines when physical clocks drift?

metadata.answer: Physical clocks drift despite NTP sync - 1ms off is possible. Logical clocks: Lamport clocks (counter incremented on events, if receive message with higher counter, advance to it+1), Vector clocks (track causal relationships). Example: server1 processes request at clock=100, sends to server2. Server2 receives with local clock=50. Use max(100, 50)+1 = 101 as new clock. Establishes happens-before relationship. At Google, TrueTime uses GPS/atomic clocks to bound uncertainty: "time T is between [t1, t2]". Google Cloud Spanner uses TrueTime for external consistency: even across regions, writes are globally ordered. Most systems use logical clocks (Lamport/Vector) for causality, NTP only ensures rough sync.

q4.label: Network Partitions & Split-Brain Prevention
q4.description: What happens during network partitions and how do you prevent split-brain?

metadata.answer: Network partition: nodes can't communicate but both alive. Without coordination: both partitions elect leaders, diverge. Split-brain: two nodes think they're leaders, data inconsistency. Prevention: quorum-based writes. If 5 nodes, need 3 votes to write. One partition has 3 (majority), other has 2 (minority). Minority partition rejects writes - no split-brain. Caveat: if network heals, reconcile diverged state. At Amazon, EC2 instance may be unreachable but running: auto-scaling group counts it as healthy until heartbeat fails. Consensus protocol helps: if node can't reach quorum, it stops participating.

q5.label: Eventual Consistency & CRDTs
q5.description: How do you design eventually consistent systems? What are CRDTs?

metadata.answer: Eventual consistency: nodes may diverge temporarily but converge given time. Example: user writes to database replica A (200ms) and replica B (via replication). For brief moment, A and B differ, then converge. Benefits: higher availability, reduced latency. CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types): data structures that merge automatically without conflicts. Example: G-Counter (grow-only counter) - each node has counter[node_id], merge by taking max of each. No conflicts. Use in collaborative editing (Google Docs): each character insert has unique ID, merge by sorting IDs. At Riak, we use CRDTs for distributed systems without central coordinator.

q6.label: Distributed Tracing & Debugging
q6.description: How do you debug issues in distributed systems across multiple services?

metadata.answer: Distributed tracing: tag each request with unique trace_id, pass through all services. Collect spans (time in each service). Jaeger/Zipkin reconstruct request flow. Example: user request to API -> calls auth service, user service, order service. All services log with trace_id=abc123. Query trace=abc123, see: API[0-10ms] -> auth[5ms] + user[8ms] + order[15ms] = 28ms total (parallel calls visible). Identify bottleneck (order service slow). At Uber, distributed tracing revealed: order lookup takes 100ms due to N+1 queries, after fix it's 5ms. With millions of requests, small per-request savings compound.

networking.label: Networking & Protocols
networking.description: TCP/IP stack, HTTP/HTTPS, DNS, connection management, protocol optimization and latency tuning.

metadata.tags: networking, tcp, http, https, dns, protocols, latency, tls

q1.label: TCP Handshake & Connection Lifecycle
q1.description: Explain TCP handshake and connection states. How do you optimize connection setup?

metadata.answer: 3-way handshake: client SYN, server SYN-ACK, client ACK. Takes 1 RTT (round-trip time). At 50ms RTT, 50ms lost just establishing connection. Optimizations: TCP Fast Open (TFO) caches token, allows data in SYN packet, saves 1 RTT. Keep-alive: reuse connections instead of new handshake each time (HTTP persistent connections). Connection pooling: client maintains pool of open connections, reuse for multiple requests. At Google, search result page needs ~100 objects (JS, CSS, images). Without keep-alive: 100 handshakes = 5 seconds. With keep-alive: ~5 requests per connection = 20 connections = 1 second. TIME-WAIT state after close: prevents packets from old connection interfering with new one. At scale, TIME-WAIT exhaustion is real problem.

q2.label: HTTP/2 & HTTP/3: Performance Improvements
q2.description: What are the performance benefits of HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 over HTTP/1.1?

metadata.answer: HTTP/1.1: 1 request per connection (head-of-line blocking). If first request takes 5 sec, others wait. HTTP/2: multiplexing - multiple requests on single connection, served in parallel. Server push: preemptively send resources client will need. Binary framing: more efficient than HTTP/1.1 text. HTTP/3: runs on QUIC instead of TCP. QUIC advantages: (1) faster handshake (1 RTT with 0-RTT if cached), (2) connection migration (change networks without reconnect - mobile switching from WiFi to LTE), (3) multiplexed streams avoid head-of-line blocking at packet level. At Netflix, switching to HTTP/3 reduced latency for mobile users by 10-15%. At Facebook, QUIC reduces stalls caused by packet loss.

q3.label: DNS Caching & Resolution
q3.description: How does DNS work and what's the impact of DNS lookups on latency?

metadata.answer: DNS: translate domain.com to IP address. Process: client queries recursive resolver (ISP), resolver queries root nameserver, TLD nameserver, authoritative nameserver, returns IP. Takes 50-100ms typically. Caching: OS caches (TTL configurable, default 60-300sec), browser caches (expires before OS), resolver caches. Optimization: increase TTL before deployment, use short TTL (60sec) for failover, implement local DNS cache. DNS prefetching: <link rel="dns-prefetch" href="//cdn.example.com"> hints browser to resolve early. At Akamai, they manage massive DNS infrastructure: anycast (same IP in multiple datacenters), geographic routing, health-check based failover.

q4.label: TLS/SSL Handshake & Certificate Management
q4.description: How does TLS encryption work and how do you optimize TLS handshake?

metadata.answer: TLS 1.2: key exchange (1 RTT) + record encryption. TLS 1.3: optimized to 1 RTT (0 RTT with resumption). Handshake: ClientHello sends supported ciphers + ephemeral key, ServerHello picks cipher + sends cert + ephemeral key, client/server derive shared secret, symmetric encryption starts. Certificate: proves server identity (signed by trusted CA). At scale: certificate rotation, certificate pinning (pin expected cert to prevent MITM), hardware accelerators for crypto. Session resumption: reuse keys from prior sessions, skip handshake. At Cloudflare, they optimized TLS at scale: served from edge datacenters close to users, reducing handshake latency. Mutual TLS (mTLS): both client and server authenticate, used in microservices.

q5.label: Load Balancing & Connection Pooling
q5.description: How do you distribute traffic and manage connection pools at scale?

metadata.answer: Load balancing: distribute requests across backends. Layer 4 (TCP): hash on source IP + port, stateful. Layer 7 (HTTP): hash on cookie/URL, can inspect headers. Algorithms: round-robin, least-connections (pick backend with fewest active), random (simple, works at scale), weighted (give more to powerful servers). Connection pooling: client library maintains pool, reuse connections. Size: typically 10-100 connections depending on service. At Twitter, they use Layer 7 LB to route based on user ID: same user always goes to same backend, cache warming. Health checks: active probing, remove unhealthy backends. Circuit breaker: stop routing if backend consistently fails.

q6.label: Congestion Control & Flow Control
q6.description: How do TCP congestion control and flow control prevent network overload?

metadata.answer: Flow control: receiver tells sender how much data to send (TCP window). Sender can't exceed window, prevents receiver buffer overflow. Congestion control: detect network congestion (packet loss), reduce sending rate. AIMD (Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease): increase congestion window by 1 MSS each RTT (additive), on loss divide by 2 (multiplicative). BBR (Google): model available bandwidth, send data at that rate, probes for more capacity periodically. More responsive than AIMD. TCP_NODELAY: disable Nagle's algorithm (small packets delayed to batch), useful for low-latency apps like multiplayer games. At Pinterest, they switched to BBR to reduce latency for slower networks.

containerization.label: Containerization & Orchestration
containerization.description: Docker, container security, Kubernetes architecture, service mesh and container networking.

metadata.tags: docker, containers, kubernetes, service-mesh, security, namespaces, orchestration

q1.label: Docker Internals: Namespaces & Cgroups
q1.description: How does Docker isolate containers? Explain namespaces and cgroups.

metadata.answer: Namespaces: isolated view of system resources. Process namespace: container sees only its processes (PID 1 is app, not init). Network namespace: own network stack (eth0, routes). IPC namespace: inter-process communication isolated. Mount namespace: own filesystem. Docker uses all namespaces to make container think it's full OS. Cgroups: limit resource usage. Memory cgroup: limit container to 512MB, OOM-kill if exceeds. CPU cgroup: limit to 2 cores via CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler). I/O cgroup: limit disk bandwidth to 100MB/s. Difference from VMs: containers share kernel, much lighter (MB vs GB). At Google, every application runs in container, billions deployed daily using cgroups for isolation and efficiency.

q2.label: Container Networking & Service Discovery
q2.description: How do containers discover and communicate with each other?

metadata.answer: Bridge networking: default Docker mode, containers on same bridge have network. But at scale, hundreds of hosts, containers need to find each other. Service discovery: DNS resolves service name to all replicas. Kubernetes: service-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local resolves to virtual IP (clusterIP), load-balanced across all pod IPs. CNI plugins: Flannel (simple overlay), Weave (mesh), Calico (BGP). Calico advantage: no tunnel overhead, scales to thousands of hosts. Service mesh (Istio/Linkerd): layer on top of container networking, handles retries, timeouts, circuit breakers, mTLS. At Spotify, they use Calico for networking across 1000+ hosts, Istio for traffic control (A/B testing, gradual rollouts).

q3.label: Kubernetes Resource Management & Limits
q3.description: How do you define resource requests and limits? What happens when pods exceed limits?

metadata.answer: Resource requests: tell scheduler how much CPU/memory pod needs. Scheduler only schedules if node has space. Limits: hard cap on resource usage. Pod exceeds memory limit: OOM-kill (evicted). Exceeds CPU limit: throttled (rate-limited). Best practice: requests = expected usage, limits = 2-3x requests (headroom for spikes). Quality of Service (QoS): Guaranteed (requests==limits), Burstable (requests<limits), BestEffort (no limits). Eviction: when node runs low on memory, kubelet evicts pods with worst QoS in order: BestEffort, Burstable, Guaranteed. At Amazon, resource limits prevent noisy neighbor (one pod using all CPU starving others). Vertical Pod Autoscaler watches usage, recommends ideal requests over time.

q4.label: Service Mesh Patterns & Benefits
q4.description: What does a service mesh do and how does it improve reliability?

metadata.answer: Service mesh (Istio/Linkerd): runs sidecar proxy beside each pod. All traffic flows through proxy (transparent to app). Proxies form mesh: can implement retries, timeouts, circuit breakers, rate limiting without changing app code. mTLS: mesh automatically encrypts pod-to-pod communication. Observability: proxies see all traffic, report metrics/logs. Canary deployments: route 5% traffic to new version, monitor, gradually increase. At Airbnb, service mesh enabled canary releases: deploy new version to 5% of traffic, if latency p99 stays low, route more. If p99 spikes, immediate rollback. Downsides: adds latency (extra proxy hop), memory overhead (sidecar per pod). Cloud providers like Google Cloud Run abstract this away.

q5.label: Container Security Scanning & Admission Control
q5.description: How do you enforce security policies in containerized environments?

metadata.answer: Image scanning: before push to registry, scan for CVEs (known vulnerabilities). If high-severity CVE found, quarantine or reject. Admission control: webhook that intercepts pod creation, enforces policies. Example: deny pods running as root, deny privileged containers, require image from approved registry. At Microsoft, they enforce: (1) images signed by trusted builder, (2) no privileged mode, (3) no raw sockets (prevent protocol attacks), (4) resource limits required. Pod security policies or Pod security standards (Kubernetes): enforces restrictions. RBAC: role-based access control, service accounts, who can create pods. Network policies: restrict which pods can talk to which (default allow -> default deny + explicit allows).

q6.label: Multi-Region Deployments & Disaster Recovery
q6.description: How do you deploy and manage containers across multiple regions for high availability?

metadata.answer: Multi-region: each region runs independent Kubernetes cluster. Traffic routed based on latency/geography via global load balancer. Data replication: databases replicate to each region (RPO/RTO targets). Canary regions: deploy new version to small region first, test in production traffic, then to all regions. Disaster recovery: if entire region fails, traffic reroutes to other regions (planned downtime minimal). State management: use distributed databases (Google Spanner, CockroachDB) or event sourcing. Backup: snapshots of persistent volumes, stored in separate region. At Netflix, they architect for region failure: any region can go down, remaining regions absorb traffic. Chaos engineering: regularly kill regions in staging, verify failover works.

architecture_hw.label: Architecture - Hardware
architecture_hw.description: Low-level hardware, CPU instruction sets (x86/ARM), and GPU compute logic.

metadata.tags: hardware, cpu, instruction-sets, x86, arm, gpu, cache, memory-hierarchy

q1.label: CPU Cache Hierarchy & Cache Coherency
q1.description: Explain L1/L2/L3 caches and how they impact performance.

metadata.answer: CPU caches organize in hierarchy: L1 (32KB, 4 cycles latency), L2 (256KB, 12 cycles), L3 (8MB, 40 cycles), main memory (100+ cycles). Data moves between levels as needed. Cache line: 64 bytes, entire line fetches/evicts together. Temporal locality: reuse same data soon (keep in L1). Spatial locality: access nearby data (cache line is beneficial). Cache coherency (multi-core): if CPU1 modifies data, CPU2 sees update. MESI protocol: Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid states track cache line ownership. False sharing: two threads modify different variables on same cache line, causes expensive invalidations. At Intel, they size L3 cache large to reduce DRAM accesses; each miss costs 100+ cycles.

q2.label: Instruction-Level Parallelism & Pipelining
q2.description: How do modern CPUs execute multiple instructions concurrently?

metadata.answer: Pipelining: break instruction into stages (fetch, decode, execute, memory, write-back). Multiple instructions in pipeline simultaneously, each at different stage. 5-stage pipeline @ 1GHz: completes 1 instruction/cycle (ILP=1). Out-of-order execution: execute independent instructions out of order, reorder results. Example: load r1 from memory (slow), then add r2+r3 (fast) - execute add while load pending. Superscalar: multiple execution units, execute 2-4 instructions/cycle. Branch prediction: guess which instruction comes next (90%+ accuracy), speculative execution. When prediction wrong, flush pipeline. Spectre/Meltdown exploit branch predictor speculatively access privileged memory. At AMD, Ryzen 7 has 4-issue superscalar, executes up to 4 instructions/cycle per core.

q3.label: x86 vs ARM Architecture Trade-offs
q3.description: Compare x86 and ARM architectures in terms of power, performance, and instruction sets.

metadata.answer: x86 (Intel, AMD): CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computing), rich instruction set, variable-length instructions. Power-hungry (15W+ per core). Strong compatibility, lots of software. x86-64: 64-bit extension. ARM (mobile, Apple Silicon): RISC (Reduced Instruction Set), simple instructions, fixed-length (32-bit). Low power (2-5W per core), long battery life. ARM64 (AArch64): 64-bit. Trade-off: x86 complex hardware, simpler compiler. ARM simple hardware, complex compiler. Apple M1: ARM ISA, custom high-performance cores + efficiency cores, beats Intel/AMD on power efficiency. At Google, they design custom ARM chips (Tensor) for ML workloads: specialized instructions for matrix multiply, energy efficient.

q4.label: GPU Architecture & Parallel Computing
q4.description: How do GPUs differ from CPUs and why are they effective for parallel workloads?

metadata.answer: GPU: thousands of small cores optimized for parallel computation. Unlike CPU (8-16 cores), GPU has 1000+ cores. Each core slower than CPU core, but massive parallelism. Execution model: SIMD (Single Instruction Multiple Data) - all cores execute same instruction on different data. Memory: high-bandwidth (600GB/s) but higher latency than CPU cache. Good for compute-to-memory ratio (1000:1 operations per byte). Terrible for random access patterns. NVIDIA CUDA: popular GPU programming. Blocks and threads: organize 1000s threads into blocks, blocks into grid. Warp: 32 threads execute in lockstep (SIMD). Divergence: if threads take different paths (if branch), performance degrades. At Meta, they use GPUs for recommendation ranking: each candidate scored in parallel on GPU, 10x faster than CPU.

q5.label: NUMA & Memory Affinity
q5.description: What is NUMA and how does it affect performance in multi-socket systems?

metadata.answer: NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access): in multi-socket systems, each socket has local memory. CPU accessing local memory: fast (50ns). Accessing remote socket memory: slow (200ns). 4x difference! Thread should access memory on same socket (locality). OS scheduler tries to place threads near their memory. Explicit NUMA control: numactl pin thread to socket. Intel: use hwloc library to query topology and pin threads. Interleaving: spread memory across sockets, each thread sees same latency but contention increases. At LinkedIn, they run 2-socket systems with 100GB RAM total. Bad NUMA affinity: each query accesses both sockets, 4x latency penalty. Good affinity: each thread operates on its socket's data.

q6.label: CPU Frequency Scaling & Power Management
q6.description: How do CPUs manage power and frequency? What's the trade-off?

metadata.answer: Power = Capacitance × Voltage² × Frequency. Doubling frequency without raising voltage saves power. Modern CPUs dynamically adjust frequency (P-states): idle cores slow down, active cores speed up. Turbo boost: temporary 10-15% frequency increase on fast cores (within power budget). Cost: higher temperature, more power draw. Thermal throttling: if temp exceeds threshold, reduce frequency to cool down. Battery vs wall power: mobile devices lower frequency to extend battery. Intel Alder Lake: hybrid architecture (P-cores + E-cores) - efficient cores run background tasks at low power, performance cores turbo on demand. At AWS, they use RAPL (Running Average Power Limit) to cap per-core power, prevent hotspots, ensure predictable power.

basics_os.label: Basics / OS - Kernel
basics_os.description: Operating system internals: scheduling, file systems, and memory management.

metadata.tags: os, kernel, scheduling, filesystem, memory-management, processes, threads

q1.label: Process vs Thread & Context Switching
q1.description: Explain the differences and explain context switch overhead.

metadata.answer: Process: independent execution context, own memory (heap, stack), file descriptors. Context switch: save CPU registers, load new process registers (~10 microseconds). Thread: lightweight, shared process memory. Context switch faster (~1 microsecond). Cost of switching: CPU cache cold (L1/L2 miss penalty), TLB miss (virtual to physical translation). If switching between processes on different cores, NUMA implications. Threads preferred over processes when possible (shared memory, less overhead). Example: web server with 10K connections, use thread pool (10 threads) reusing threads instead of 10K processes. Goroutines (Go): userspace concurrency, 1000s/thread, avoid OS context switch overhead. At Netflix, they use thread pools with work-stealing queues to minimize context switches.

q2.label: CPU Scheduling Algorithms
q2.description: Explain scheduling algorithms: Round Robin, Priority, CFS.

metadata.answer: Round Robin: each process gets time slice (5-10ms), switches out. Simple, fair, but high context switch overhead. Priority scheduling: higher priority runs first. Starvation risk: low priority waits forever if high priority always ready. Multilevel feedback: process priority adjusts based on behavior (I/O waits, gets higher priority). CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler, Linux): virtual runtime tracks how much wall-clock time process should have ran (fair share). Process with least virtual runtime runs next. Nice values adjust weight (nice=-20 high priority, nice=19 low priority). O(1) insertion/deletion with red-black tree. At Google, they use custom scheduler: consider NUMA locality, power consumption, cache affinity alongside fairness.
